reason: Chronologic age within an already ≥80 cohort; older age modestly increases AKI susceptibility. Likely limited incremental signal due to restricted range and being dominated by perioperative physiology and comorbidity variables.

Biologic sex can correlate with baseline muscle mass/creatinine kinetics and AKI risk. In a sparse model it is a weak, indirect predictor and often redundant with body size and illness-severity features.
Race category may proxy social/biologic differences but is not a direct renal physiology marker. In sparse selection it is low-yield and largely redundant with comorbidities and perioperative course.

Race category can correlate with baseline creatinine generation and CKD burden, potentially affecting creatinine trajectory even when using a ratio. Still indirect and likely weaker than direct comorbidity/physiology signals, so low priority.
Race category with limited direct mechanistic link to postoperative AKI beyond confounding. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.
Race category; any association with renal outcomes is indirect and likely unstable due to small counts. Low priority for sparse selection.
Race category; primarily a demographic proxy rather than a near-term renal risk signal. Likely low prevalence and low unique contribution.
Catch-all race category with heterogeneous meaning and weak mechanistic specificity. Poor candidate for sparse inclusion.
Ethnicity is a demographic proxy that may reflect confounding rather than renal pathophysiology. Typically adds little unique predictive value compared with comorbidities and perioperative variables.
Body size measure; may relate to creatinine generation but the outcome is a creatinine ratio, reducing relevance. Likely redundant with weight and offers minimal unique AKI prediction signal.

Body mass can influence volume of distribution, hemodilution, and dosing, which may affect creatinine changes post-op. Still indirect and often redundant with fluid balance/hemodynamics, so modest priority.

Long-term cardiovascular risk marker with little direct connection to acute postoperative renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxic exposure. Very unlikely to survive sparse-model screening for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Diabetes is a strong risk factor for underlying CKD/microvascular disease and postoperative AKI after cardiac surgery. Provides meaningful baseline susceptibility information that is not fully captured by immediate post-op vitals alone.

Indicator of diabetes control (likely glycemic control status) which may correlate with chronic end-organ vulnerability. Adds some nuance beyond diabetes but is often noisy/definition-dependent and less predictive than diabetes itself.
Chronic atherosclerotic risk factor with weak direct linkage to acute creatinine rise over 60 hours. Likely redundant with other comorbidity burden measures and low yield for sparse selection.

Hypertension is associated with CKD and reduced renal reserve, increasing AKI risk post-cardiac surgery. Useful baseline susceptibility signal, though partially redundant with diabetes and other comorbidity/physiology features.

History of infective endocarditis suggests prior severe infection/valve disease and possible baseline frailty. It is an indirect AKI risk factor and likely dominated by perioperative hemodynamics/labs in a sparse model.

Chronic lung disease reflects baseline cardiopulmonary reserve and risk of hypoxemia/ventilator dependence. It is a nonspecific severity marker with limited unique signal for creatinine ratio once ICU physiology is included.

Home oxygen use is a proxy for advanced pulmonary disease and overall frailty. Predictive value for AKI is indirect and typically redundant with postoperative oxygenation/vasopressor/ventilation features.

Bronchodilator therapy mainly indicates obstructive lung disease treatment rather than renal physiology. It is a weak, behavior/treatment proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection for creatinine ratio.
Sleep apnea indicates chronic intermittent hypoxia and cardiometabolic risk. For Hour-60 creatinine ratio it is distal and usually overwhelmed by perioperative hemodynamics, fluids, and labs.

Pneumonia reflects active infection/inflammation and can drive hypotension, nephrotoxic antibiotics, and multiorgan dysfunction. It can add some unique AKI risk signal beyond generic comorbidity, though still indirect.
IV drug abuse is a social/behavioral comorbidity that may correlate with infections or liver disease. It is not a direct renal signal and is likely too sparse/noisy to help a linear sparse model.
Depression is a baseline psychosocial comorbidity with minimal mechanistic linkage to acute postoperative AKI. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value compared with physiologic ICU features.

Alcohol use can correlate with liver dysfunction, malnutrition, and perioperative instability. It is an indirect proxy and often redundant with explicit liver disease and postoperative lab/hemodynamic signals.

Liver disease indicates impaired perfusion reserve, inflammation, and altered drug handling, all associated with postoperative AKI risk. It can contribute unique baseline risk information, though still less direct than renal/hemodynamic markers.

Immunosuppression increases infection risk and may reflect complex comorbidity burden. Its effect on creatinine ratio is indirect and likely mediated through postoperative complications captured by ICU features.

Prior mediastinal radiation suggests complex cardiac pathology and higher-risk surgery. It is a contextual risk marker but not a direct renal physiology signal and may be redundant with operative/ICU severity variables.
Cancer reflects chronic illness burden and potential nephrotoxic exposures. For Hour-60 creatinine ratio it is a broad proxy and typically weaker than immediate postoperative physiologic predictors.

Peripheral artery disease is a marker of systemic atherosclerosis and renal microvascular vulnerability, strongly associated with postoperative AKI risk. It provides relatively unique baseline susceptibility information that can persist even after accounting for ICU course.

Thoracic aortic disease implies more complex surgery (e.g., longer bypass/circulatory arrest) and embolic risk, which can precipitate AKI. It is a meaningful baseline/operative-risk proxy but still indirect compared with direct renal/hemodynamic measures.
Syncope is a nonspecific symptom that may reflect arrhythmia or valvular disease but is not directly tied to renal injury. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

History of unresponsive state is a nonspecific neurologic comorbidity marker. It has little direct mechanistic link to postoperative AKI and is likely dominated by perioperative hemodynamics and renal/lab signals in a sparse model.

Cerebrovascular disease reflects systemic atherosclerosis/frailty and higher perioperative risk. It is an indirect proxy for illness burden and usually adds limited unique value for creatinine trajectory once cardiac function/hemodynamics and baseline renal risk are accounted for.

Pre-op hemoglobin captures anemia and oxygen delivery reserve, which can contribute to renal hypoxia during/after surgery. It can add some unique baseline risk signal, but is still indirect and often correlated with frailty/nutrition/bleeding risk.

Pre-op albumin is a marker of nutrition, inflammation, and oncotic pressure affecting fluid shifts. It can modestly predict AKI risk but is largely a general vulnerability proxy and may be redundant with other severity/frailty measures.

Baseline bilirubin reflects hepatic dysfunction/cholestasis and overall organ reserve. It is an indirect predictor of postoperative AKI and is typically weaker than integrated liver/renal severity scores (e.g., MELD) for sparse selection.

HbA1c reflects chronic glycemic control and diabetic microvascular risk, which increases susceptibility to AKI. It provides baseline risk information but is distal to the 60-hour creatinine change and may be redundant with known diabetes/CKD-related features not shown here.

HIT antibody status mainly indicates a coagulation/anticoagulation management issue rather than renal physiology. Any association with creatinine ratio would be indirect (complications/therapy changes) and unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

MELD score (including creatinine and bilirubin/INR components) summarizes baseline hepatorenal dysfunction and physiologic reserve. It is a strong pre-op risk integrator for postoperative AKI and likely adds more unique predictive value than bilirubin or albumin alone.

BNP reflects cardiac filling pressures and heart failure severity, which strongly relates to renal congestion and low forward flow after cardiac surgery. It is a relatively specific upstream driver of AKI risk and can add unique value beyond generic comorbidity flags.

5-meter walk test is a frailty/functional status measure associated with postoperative complications. It is a broad vulnerability proxy and typically less directly predictive of creatinine ratio than cardio-renal physiologic markers.

Prior cardiovascular intervention indicates advanced coronary/vascular disease and procedural complexity. It is indirect and likely redundant with more specific prior CABG/PCI variables and perioperative course features.

Prior CABG suggests redo sternotomy risk and potentially longer/complex surgery, which can increase AKI risk. It may add some unique procedural-risk signal but remains indirect relative to hemodynamic/renal biomarkers.

History of prior cardiac surgery captures redo status and operative complexity, which can affect renal outcomes via longer bypass time/bleeding. Useful but overlaps with prior CABG and other intervention history, limiting sparse-model priority.

Prior PCI reflects coronary disease burden but is generally less tied to operative complexity than redo surgery. It is an indirect risk marker and often redundant with broader cardiovascular history variables.

PCI during the same episode may imply recent contrast exposure and acuity, which can contribute to AKI risk. Still, it is a coarse proxy (dose/timing unknown) and may be dominated by direct renal function and perioperative hemodynamics.
Stent history indicates coronary disease and antiplatelet considerations but has no direct renal signal. Any predictive value is indirect and likely redundant with other PCI/history features.

Represents chronic cardiac disease history and recency of prior procedures. May weakly stratify baseline risk/complexity but is indirect for AKI and likely dominated by post-op hemodynamics/labs in a sparse model.

Counts prior cardiac procedures, a proxy for comorbidity burden and surgical complexity. Predictive value for creatinine_ratio is indirect and likely redundant with more proximal physiologic/renal signals.

Recent ACE inhibitor exposure can predispose to perioperative hypotension and reduced renal autoregulation. It can add some unique baseline susceptibility signal, but is still a pre-op proxy and often outweighed by post-op perfusion/vasopressor features.
Recent P2Y12 inhibitor use mainly reflects antiplatelet management and bleeding risk. Any association with AKI is indirect (transfusion/bleeding pathways) and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Amiodarone use is a marker of arrhythmia burden and cardiac disease severity. It has no direct renal signal and is mostly a comorbidity proxy, so low priority for a sparse model.
Anticoagulant use reflects atrial fibrillation/VTE risk and may relate to bleeding/transfusion. The pathway to AKI is indirect and likely redundant with direct post-op bleeding/hemodynamic variables.
Aspirin use is common and primarily indicates CAD prevention. It provides minimal unique information for predicting postoperative creatinine_ratio and is unlikely to be selected sparsely.

Beta-blocker use reflects baseline cardiac disease and may influence perioperative hemodynamics. However it is a broad proxy and typically dominated by measured post-op blood pressure/vasopressor requirements.
Longer-term beta-blocker therapy is largely redundant with the simpler pre_op_beta_blockers indicator. Adds little incremental signal for AKI risk beyond other comorbidity/hemodynamic features.
CCB therapy is a chronic medication marker (HTN/angina) with weak, indirect linkage to AKI. Low uniqueness and likely redundant with comorbidity and perioperative physiology.
GPIIb/IIIa inhibitor use is uncommon and mainly reflects acute coronary syndrome management and bleeding risk. Any AKI association is indirect and noisy, making it a poor sparse-model candidate.

Pre-op inotrope requirement indicates low cardiac output/hemodynamic instability, a strong precursor to renal hypoperfusion and postoperative AKI. This is a relatively unique baseline severity signal likely to persist beyond immediate post-op noise.
Statin/lipid-lowering therapy is prevalent and mainly reflects chronic cardiovascular risk management. It has minimal direct or unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio in a sparse model.
Chronic nitrate therapy indicates angina/CAD but does not directly inform renal injury risk. Likely redundant with broader CAD history and perioperative hemodynamics.

IV nitrates suggest active ischemia/heart failure management and potential hemodynamic lability. Still an indirect proxy for AKI and typically dominated by direct post-op perfusion/vasopressor signals.
Other antianginal therapy is a nonspecific chronic medication marker. It adds little unique information for postoperative creatinine_ratio beyond general cardiac comorbidity indicators.

Pre-op steroid exposure may reflect chronic inflammatory disease and can affect glucose/infection risk. It is an indirect, low-specificity AKI signal and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers in a sparse model.

Recent direct thrombin inhibitor use mainly reflects anticoagulation management and bleeding risk. It has little direct mechanistic link to postoperative creatinine rise and is mostly a care-context proxy.

Warfarin use indicates chronic anticoagulation (e.g., AF/mechanical valve) and periop reversal/bleeding considerations. It is not a direct renal-risk marker and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Factor Xa inhibitor exposure is primarily a medication-history/bleeding-risk variable. It is an indirect proxy and typically redundant with broader comorbidity/severity features rather than kidney-specific risk.
Thrombin inhibitor use within 5 days is another anticoagulation-history indicator. It is weakly related to AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus physiologic/renal trajectory variables.

Statin use is a marker of atherosclerotic burden and chronic cardiovascular risk. Any renal-protective/anti-inflammatory association is modest and likely confounded, so it is a low-priority sparse feature.

Pre-op cath implies contrast exposure and higher CAD workup intensity, which can contribute to pre-op renal stress. It can add some unique AKI risk signal beyond anatomy alone, but remains indirect and confounded by indication.

Known coronary anatomy disease indicates underlying CAD burden. It is a broad comorbidity marker and is likely redundant with more granular CAD severity (e.g., number of diseased vessels) and periop physiology.

Extent of CAD captures cardiovascular reserve and likelihood of complex surgery/low-output states that predispose to AKI. More informative than a binary CAD flag, but still an upstream risk factor rather than a direct renal signal.

Aortic valve disease (often AS) relates to baseline hemodynamics and periop low-output/hypoperfusion risk. It can contribute unique risk context for AKI, though much of its effect may be mediated by postoperative hemodynamics.

Mitral valve disease reflects chronic heart failure/pulmonary congestion physiology. It is a weaker, more heterogeneous predictor of renal hypoperfusion than aortic stenosis and often redundant with hemodynamic features.

Tricuspid disease can indicate right-sided failure and venous congestion, which is relevant to cardiorenal syndrome and postoperative AKI. It may add some unique signal beyond left-sided lesions, but remains indirect.
Pulmonic valve disease is uncommon and typically not a primary driver of postoperative renal dysfunction in this setting. Likely sparse, noisy, and dominated by direct hemodynamic/renal markers.

Number of prior cardiac surgeries is a strong marker of operative complexity, adhesions, longer bypass/cross-clamp, and higher risk of low-output/bleeding—all AKI precursors. It is a relatively unique pre-op risk feature likely to survive sparse selection.

Case status (e.g., elective/urgent/emergent) is a general severity/acuity indicator. It can correlate with AKI risk but is nonspecific and often redundant with physiologic instability and procedural complexity variables.

Cancellation reflects scheduling/logistics or pre-op instability but is not a physiologic biomarker. It is likely sparse/noisy and a weak proxy unlikely to add stable predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Conversion (e.g., minimally invasive/robotic to open) signals intraoperative complexity/complications that can increase AKI risk via longer ischemic time, bleeding, and hemodynamic instability. Useful as a compact severity marker but indirect and often redundant with more direct perfusion/renal signals.

Robotic approach mainly reflects case selection and operative approach rather than renal physiology. Predictive value for creatinine_ratio is likely weak and mediated by other factors (procedure type, bypass exposure, hemodynamics).

CABG indicates a procedure class with typical CPB/off-pump patterns and perioperative hemodynamic risk that can influence AKI. Moderately useful for baseline risk stratification but indirect and partly redundant with more granular intraop perfusion variables.

Valve surgery often entails longer CPB/cross-clamp and higher risk of low-output states, which can drive postoperative AKI. Helpful as a broad risk marker but not a direct renal signal and may overlap with other procedure indicators.

Additional cardiac procedures imply greater operative complexity and physiologic stress, increasing AKI risk. Moderately informative but heterogeneous and largely a proxy for severity rather than a specific renal mechanism.

AF ablation/maze adds operative time but usually contributes limited incremental renal risk compared with major aortic/valve procedures. Likely low unique value in a sparse model once overall procedure complexity is captured.

Aortic procedures are strongly associated with AKI risk due to longer CPB, hypothermia/circulatory arrest, embolic burden, and major hemodynamic perturbations. High-yield categorical predictor with relatively unique signal within procedure-type features.
Concurrent non-cardiac procedures mainly reflect complexity and perioperative exposure rather than kidney-specific physiology. Likely weak and heterogeneous, so low priority for sparse selection.

Appropriate prophylactic antibiotic choice is a quality/process metric and not a direct driver of early postoperative creatinine changes. Any association would be indirect and likely negligible for Hour-60 creatinine_ratio.
Timing of prophylactic antibiotics is primarily a compliance/process indicator. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value for AKI compared with hemodynamics, perfusion, and renal biomarkers.
Antibiotic discontinuation order reflects stewardship/process and clinician behavior rather than renal injury physiology. Low likelihood of meaningful independent prediction of creatinine_ratio.

An extra prophylactic dose can proxy longer operative duration or blood loss, which may correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and noisy, and typically dominated by direct duration/perfusion/instability measures if available.

Lowest temperature reflects hypothermia strategy and may proxy complex CPB/aortic work (and potential circulatory arrest), which relates to AKI risk. Moderately useful but partly redundant with aortic procedure/CPB-related indicators.

Nadir hemoglobin captures intraoperative anemia/hemodilution/bleeding, which can impair renal oxygen delivery and is linked to postoperative AKI. Strong, relatively direct physiologic insult marker likely to survive sparse selection.

Intraoperative hyperglycemia reflects stress response/diabetes and illness severity, with only indirect linkage to early AKI. Likely redundant with comorbidity and severity variables and lower priority for a sparse model.

Use of cardiopulmonary/coronary perfusion indicates specific perfusion strategies and often more complex surgery, which can affect renal perfusion and inflammatory load. Moderately informative but indirect and may overlap with procedure-type and temperature signals.

Aortic arterial cannulation is the common default for CPB and mainly reflects surgical approach. It has at most weak incremental AKI signal beyond bypass time/arrest complexity, so low sparse-model priority.

Femoral arterial cannulation often indicates peripheral cannulation, vascular disease, or emergent/redo contexts that can increase embolic/low-flow risk. It may add some unique risk-context signal beyond CPB time, but remains indirect.

Axillary cannulation is typically used in complex aortic surgery and may correlate with circulatory arrest and altered perfusion strategies. Provides moderate incremental context for renal hypoperfusion risk but is partly redundant with arrest/DHCA variables.

Innominate cannulation is another marker of complex arch/aortic procedures and selective perfusion strategies. Moderately informative as a procedure-complexity proxy, but overlaps with circulatory arrest/DHCA time.
Other arterial sites are heterogeneous and mainly encode uncommon surgical approaches. The mixed meaning makes it a weak, noisy predictor compared with explicit CPB/arrest duration features.

Femoral venous cannulation suggests peripheral cannulation or access limitations and is mostly a procedural-context marker. Likely redundant with overall case complexity and provides limited direct renal physiology signal.
Jugular venous cannulation is uncommon and typically reflects access strategy rather than renal perfusion. Adds little unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.
Right atrial venous cannulation is standard for many CPB cases and thus has low discriminative value. Minimal incremental AKI prediction beyond CPB time and arrest variables.

Left atrial venous cannulation is procedure-specific (e.g., certain valve/redo contexts) but remains an indirect proxy. Likely dominated by stronger indicators of operative complexity and perfusion time.
Pulmonary venous cannulation is rare and highly heterogeneous in indication. Too infrequent/noisy to be a reliable sparse-model predictor of postoperative creatinine trajectory.
Bicaval cannulation reflects specific intracardiac procedures and surgical technique. It is an indirect complexity marker and generally redundant with CPB duration and arrest-related variables.
Other venous sites are heterogeneous and primarily encode idiosyncratic operative choices. Low interpretability and likely weak, unstable predictive contribution.

Total CPB duration captures exposure to nonpulsatile flow, inflammation/hemolysis, and cumulative renal hypoperfusion risk. It is a strong, well-established, nonredundant operative predictor of AKI and should be top-tier for sparse selection.

Whether circulatory arrest occurred indicates profound low-flow/ischemic exposure and major operative complexity. Strong AKI risk signal, though partially correlated with DHCA time and perfusion strategy.

Duration of DHCA directly quantifies the intensity of global ischemia/low-flow exposure, a potent driver of postoperative organ dysfunction including AKI. Provides more granular predictive signal than the binary arrest indicator and is high-priority for sparsity.

Use of cerebral perfusion reflects arch surgery strategy and may correlate with longer complex cases and altered systemic perfusion. Moderately informative as a context/proxy variable but largely redundant with arrest and CPB time.

Duration of selective cerebral perfusion reflects complex arch surgery and prolonged bypass exposure. It is an indirect AKI risk proxy and likely redundant with stronger global ischemic-time variables (e.g., cross-clamp/arrest time) in a sparse model.

Time in circulatory arrest captures profound systemic hypoperfusion/ischemia during surgery, a strong precursor to postoperative AKI. It is relatively unique and often more informative than averages of hemodynamics for sparse selection.

Binary indicator of aortic occlusion/cross-clamping reflects operative technique and case type. It is coarse and largely redundant with cross-clamp duration, so lower priority for a sparse linear model.

Cross-clamp time is a key summary of myocardial ischemic time and correlates with CPB duration, inflammatory load, and low-output states that drive AKI. It is a high-yield, widely generalizable intraop predictor likely to survive sparse screening.

Use of cerebral oximetry is mainly a monitoring/clinician-behavior marker and case-complexity proxy. It provides little direct renal physiology signal and is unlikely to add unique value beyond operative times.

Diffuse aortic calcification reflects vascular disease burden and may correlate with baseline renal vulnerability. However it is indirect and often overlaps with comorbidity/age-related risk, so modest sparse-model priority.

Whether the ascending aorta/arch was assessed is primarily a procedural/documentation and complexity proxy. It is not a direct renal insult marker and is likely redundant with other operative descriptors.

Aortic plaque assessment indicates intraop evaluation rather than a quantified physiologic exposure. As a clinician-action proxy with unclear scaling, it is low value for sparse prediction of creatinine ratio.

Change in surgical plan due to aortic condition signals complex/high-risk surgery that can increase AKI risk. Still, it is a coarse proxy and likely dominated by objective time/exposure variables (cross-clamp/arrest, transfusion).

Refusal of blood products reflects patient preference and may lead to anemia/hypoperfusion, but events are rare and heterogeneous. It is a weak, noisy predictor compared with actual transfusion amounts and operative times.

Any intraop blood product receipt summarizes bleeding/coagulopathy and hemodilution, which are associated with AKI risk. Useful but somewhat redundant with specific component volumes (RBC/FFP/platelets/cryo) for sparse selection.

RBC transfusion volume is a strong marker of bleeding severity, anemia, and inflammatory/hemolysis exposure, all linked to postoperative AKI. It often adds more specific signal than a generic 'blood products received' indicator.

FFP transfusion reflects coagulopathy and major bleeding/CPB effects, which correlate with AKI risk. It can add signal but is typically secondary to RBC volume and overall operative time in a sparse model.
Platelet transfusion indicates bleeding or platelet dysfunction after CPB. Predictive value for AKI is more indirect and often redundant with RBC/FFP and surgical complexity measures.

Cryoprecipitate use suggests severe coagulopathy/bleeding (low fibrinogen) and high-acuity surgery. It is informative when present but relatively infrequent and overlaps with other transfusion variables.

Intraoperative clotting factor administration (e.g., PCC/factor concentrates) is a marker of significant coagulopathy/bleeding management. It is an indirect severity/treatment proxy and likely redundant with transfusion volumes and operative times.

Use of aminocaproic acid is an antifibrinolytic choice reflecting surgeon/anesthesia practice and bleeding risk. It is an indirect proxy with weak, non-specific linkage to AKI compared with hemodynamics/renal markers, so low sparse-model priority.

Tranexamic acid administration is primarily a bleeding-prophylaxis practice pattern and case-type marker. Any association with creatinine rise is likely confounded and redundant with stronger severity/bleeding/transfusion signals not in this batch.

TEE performed indicates intraoperative monitoring intensity and procedural complexity. This is a clinician-behavior proxy and unlikely to add unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse linear model.

Residual aortic regurgitation can worsen forward flow and contribute to low effective perfusion. It is a plausible but indirect renal hypoperfusion signal and may be partially captured by EF/gradients, so moderate priority.

Residual mitral regurgitation can impair cardiac output and raise venous pressures, both relevant to renal perfusion/congestion. Useful but indirect and likely redundant with EF and other valve-performance metrics, so mid-tier.

Residual tricuspid regurgitation is a strong marker of right-sided failure and venous congestion, which is closely linked to postoperative AKI risk. Compared with left-sided valve findings, it can add more unique renal-relevant signal, so strong candidate.

Indicator of valve surgery type/occurrence captures baseline procedural category and complexity. It is upstream and non-physiologic, and its effect on AKI is usually mediated through bypass time/hemodynamics not represented here, so low-moderate priority.

Atrial fibrillation procedure (e.g., Maze/ablation) mainly reflects operative add-on complexity and surgeon preference. It has weak direct mechanistic linkage to renal injury and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
General anesthesia is near-universal in cardiac surgery and offers little discrimination. It provides minimal unique information for predicting creatinine_ratio, so very low priority.

PCC use signals significant bleeding/coagulopathy and aggressive resuscitation, which can correlate with hypotension, transfusion, and renal insult. It is still an indirect treatment proxy but may add unique severity information within this batch, so moderate priority.

Post-repair aortic mean gradient reflects residual stenosis/afterload and valve performance. It is an indirect contributor to perfusion and often less predictive than direct hemodynamic/renal markers, so low-moderate priority.

Paravalvular leak can cause hemolysis and reduced effective forward flow, both potentially impacting renal function. It is relatively specific but still indirect and may be uncommon, so moderate sparse-model value.

Mitral mean gradient indicates residual stenosis and left atrial pressure burden. Its renal relevance is indirect and likely weaker/redundant versus EF and regurgitation measures, so low-moderate priority.

Mitral paravalvular leak can reflect suboptimal repair with potential hemodynamic consequences and hemolysis. It may add some unique signal but remains indirect for AKI prediction, so moderate priority.

Combined mitral/tricuspid leak is a coarse composite of residual valve dysfunction. It is likely redundant with the separate mitral and tricuspid insufficiency variables (especially TR for renal congestion), so lower priority.

Post-procedure EF summarizes left ventricular systolic function and is a key determinant of cardiac output and renal perfusion after surgery. It is a relatively direct physiologic driver and broadly informative across cases, making it a strong sparse-model keeper.

Planned PCI is a care-plan/procedural intent variable rather than a physiologic renal signal. It is likely weakly related to AKI risk and largely redundant with overall cardiac disease severity and procedure complexity proxies.

Intra-aortic balloon pump use indicates perioperative low cardiac output/hemodynamic instability with risk of renal hypoperfusion. It is a strong, relatively unique severity/hemodynamics marker that can add value in a sparse model.

Cath lab assist suggests need for mechanical/adjunct support around the procedure, reflecting complexity and instability. Predictive value for creatinine rise is indirect and likely overlaps with IABP/ECMO and other shock markers.

ECMO indicates profound cardiopulmonary failure with high rates of AKI from hypoperfusion, inflammation, hemolysis, and nephrotoxic exposures. It is a top-tier, high-signal indicator likely to survive sparse selection.

Blood product transfusion volume reflects bleeding/hemodilution and inflammatory burden, and is associated with AKI risk after cardiac surgery. It is moderately useful but can be partly redundant with postop hemoglobin/hematocrit and overall illness severity.

Extubation in the OR is a proxy for uncomplicated course and better cardiopulmonary reserve. It is indirect for renal outcomes and may be dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal perfusion variables.

Re-intubation signals postoperative respiratory failure and clinical deterioration, often correlating with shock, infection, and higher AKI risk. It can add some unique trajectory information but remains an indirect severity proxy.

ICU visit is largely a workflow/triage variable and may have limited variability in this cohort of elderly cardiac surgery patients. It is a weak, non-physiologic proxy and unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.

Postoperative hemoglobin reflects anemia/hemodilution and bleeding/transfusion balance, which can influence renal oxygen delivery. It is moderately predictive but overlaps strongly with hematocrit and transfusion variables, limiting sparse-model priority.
Postoperative hematocrit is essentially redundant with postoperative hemoglobin as an anemia/hemodilution marker. In a sparse model, one of the pair would dominate, so this is lower priority.

Postoperative intubation status reflects immediate postoperative respiratory support needs and overall acuity. It is indirect for kidney injury and likely redundant with re-intubation and mechanical support/hemodynamic instability markers.

History of prior MI captures baseline ischemic cardiomyopathy risk and potential for low-output states. It is a distal, baseline comorbidity signal and often dominated by perioperative support variables (IABP/ECMO) for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Timing of prior MI is a nuanced baseline history detail with uncertain incremental value beyond the presence of prior MI and current perioperative instability. Likely low signal-to-noise for sparse selection.

Symptom timing at admission is a subjective/administrative proxy for presentation acuity rather than a direct renal risk marker. It is unlikely to add unique predictive value once perioperative course variables are included.

Symptom timing at surgery similarly reflects scheduling/clinical context more than renal physiology. It is a weak, indirect predictor and likely redundant with procedure urgency and postoperative instability markers.

Angina class reflects baseline coronary symptom burden and functional limitation. It may weakly relate to perioperative risk but is indirect for AKI and typically dominated by immediate postoperative hemodynamic support indicators.

NYHA functional class reflects baseline heart failure symptom burden and overall reserve. It is an indirect, preop severity marker and likely adds limited unique value once postoperative hemodynamics/renal signals are in the model.
Cardiogenic shock indicates profound low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion risk. It is a strong upstream driver of AKI and can add unique predictive value beyond generic comorbidity flags.

Resuscitation suggests major perioperative instability (e.g., arrest/CPR) with ischemic insult risk. Predictive but partly overlaps with shock/vasopressor/hemodynamic features, so moderate priority for sparsity.
A broad arrhythmia indicator captures rhythm instability that may affect perfusion. It is nonspecific and likely redundant with the more specific arrhythmia subtype variables in this batch.

Recent VT/VF reflects severe electrical instability and potential periods of low perfusion. It can matter for AKI risk but is episodic and often mediated through shock/resuscitation, limiting unique sparse-model value.

Sick sinus syndrome is a conduction disorder more related to pacing needs than systemic hypoperfusion. It is weakly linked to postoperative creatinine trajectory and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Atrial flutter can reduce cardiac output and prompt rate-control/anticoagulation decisions. It is generally less predictive of AKI than shock/heart failure and overlaps with AF and generic arrhythmia indicators.
Second-degree AV block mainly reflects conduction disease and pacing/monitoring needs. It is an indirect proxy with limited mechanistic linkage to renal injury compared with perfusion variables.

Third-degree AV block can cause bradycardia and low output if not paced. Some relevance via perfusion, but much of the signal is captured by pacing status and hemodynamic instability features elsewhere.

Pacemaker dependence indicates conduction disease and may correlate with perioperative complexity. It is mostly a context/procedure proxy and not a direct renal-risk biomarker, so low-to-moderate sparse priority.

Heart failure is a strong baseline determinant of renal vulnerability (cardiorenal physiology) and postoperative low-output risk. It is a high-yield comorbidity likely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Recent atrial fibrillation is common and can worsen hemodynamics and reflect atrial/ventricular disease burden. Moderately predictive but often redundant with heart failure and generic instability markers.

Preoperative pain score reflects symptom reporting and perioperative comfort rather than renal physiology. It is unlikely to provide unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and is a poor sparse-model candidate.

Cell saver volume is a proxy for surgical blood loss/transfusion avoidance and operative complexity. It may correlate with inflammation/hemolysis but is indirect and typically dominated by direct hemodynamics/labs/transfusion variables.

Heparin dose before bypass mainly reflects protocolized anticoagulation and patient size/bypass management. It has little direct relationship to postoperative renal dysfunction and is unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Protamine dose reflects heparin reversal and can be associated with hemodynamic reactions/bleeding management. It is an indirect perioperative management proxy and usually weaker than direct perfusion/renal markers for sparse selection.

Intraoperative viscoelastic coagulation testing (e.g., TEG/ROTEM) reflects bleeding/coagulopathy management rather than renal physiology. Any association with AKI is indirect and likely dominated by hemodynamics, transfusion, and perfusion variables not in this batch.
Indicator/amount of volatile anesthetic exposure is mainly an anesthesia practice marker. It has weak, indirect linkage to AKI and is redundant with the specific volatile-agent variables below.

Use of isoflurane is largely a clinician-choice/exposure variable with minimal direct renal signal. In a sparse model it is unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond overall anesthetic exposure or perfusion variables.

Sevoflurane exposure has some theoretical nephrotoxicity concerns, but clinically in cardiac surgery its incremental predictive value for AKI is usually small. Likely redundant with overall volatile use and confounded by case/anesthesia selection.
Desflurane use is primarily a practice pattern marker and not a direct kidney injury precursor. Low likelihood of surviving sparse selection for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.
Other volatile agent use is heterogeneous and mostly captures documentation/practice variation. Too nonspecific and indirect to contribute uniquely to AKI prediction.
Pre-bypass volatile use reflects early anesthetic management and is temporally distant from the 60h creatinine outcome. Any signal is indirect and likely redundant with overall volatile exposure.

During-bypass volatile use may correlate with bypass management, but it is still an exposure/proxy rather than a renal perfusion marker. Sparse models would typically prefer direct hemodynamic/renal variables over this.

Post-bypass volatile use is closer to immediate postoperative physiology but remains a weak proxy for illness severity/anesthesia choice. Limited unique value for later creatinine ratio compared with direct perfusion/AKI markers.
Maintenance volatile use summarizes anesthetic strategy rather than kidney injury mechanisms. Likely redundant with other volatile-use indicators and not a top sparse-model keeper.

Dexmedetomidine infusion reflects sedation strategy and may correlate with hemodynamic stability, but renal effects are indirect. More of a clinician-behavior proxy than a direct AKI precursor, so low sparse priority.

Propofol infusion is an anesthesia exposure variable with indirect links to hypotension and case complexity. Likely confounded and redundant with other anesthetic choices, so not a strong sparse-model feature.

Midazolam infusion mainly captures sedation practice and patient/anesthesia preference. It has little direct mechanistic connection to postoperative creatinine rise and is unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Total intraoperative insulin dose is a proxy for hyperglycemia/diabetes severity and stress response, which are associated with AKI risk. It may add some unique signal in this batch because it captures metabolic stress not otherwise represented here.

Pre-anesthesia systolic BP reflects baseline hemodynamic reserve and chronic hypertension, which influence renal vulnerability. It can contribute modest unique baseline risk information, though it is temporally distant and not a direct AKI marker.

Pre-anesthesia diastolic BP is another baseline hemodynamic/chronic vascular tone marker but is often less informative than systolic for perioperative risk. Likely partially redundant with systolic BP and thus lower sparse priority.

Pre-induction heart rate reflects baseline sympathetic tone/fitness and may correlate with frailty or hemodynamic reserve. It is an indirect, pre-op severity marker and is likely dominated by more direct post-op perfusion/vasopressor and renal signals for Hour-60 creatinine.

Use of a PA catheter is largely a clinician-choice proxy for perceived cardiac complexity/instability. It may add some risk stratification but is non-physiologic and typically redundant with actual hemodynamics and vasoactive requirements.

Maximum core temperature captures perioperative hyperthermia/rewarming and possibly inflammatory response. It is a weak, nonspecific AKI predictor and usually less informative than direct perfusion, lactate, urine output, or creatinine trajectory features.

Inhaled nitric oxide use suggests pulmonary hypertension/RV strain and complex cardiopulmonary management. It is an indirect treatment proxy and likely redundant with RV function and vasopressor/inotrope needs for predicting renal dysfunction.

Total intraoperative crystalloid reflects volume loading/hemodilution and can relate to renal congestion or hypotension management. It can contribute unique signal about fluid exposure but is confounded by practice patterns and overlaps with net fluid balance and transfusion variables (if present elsewhere).

Synthetic colloid volume captures exposure to agents historically associated with AKI risk and also marks hemodynamic instability requiring volume expansion. It may add some unique predictive value versus crystalloid, but usage is practice-dependent and may be sparse/noisy.

Albumin volume reflects intravascular volume expansion strategy and can correlate with capillary leak/vasoplegia and fluid responsiveness. Moderately useful as an exposure marker, but partially redundant with overall fluid totals and severity-of-illness management patterns.
Intraoperative glucose trough reflects glycemic control and metabolic stress. It is an indirect marker and typically weaker than direct renal perfusion/toxin exposure signals for sparse selection.

Intraoperative vasodilator use suggests hypertension management or afterload reduction and can relate to periods of lower perfusion pressure. It may carry some signal about hemodynamic lability but is a treatment proxy and often redundant with measured MAP minima and vasopressor requirements.

Intra-procedure EEG monitoring mainly reflects anesthetic/neurologic monitoring intensity and case complexity. It has little direct mechanistic link to AKI and is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.

Pre-op/intra-op TEE use is a procedural/monitoring choice indicating complex valve/aortic pathology or hemodynamic concern. It is mostly a proxy and redundant with the actual echo-derived functional measures included in this batch.

Pre-incision LVEF summarizes baseline LV systolic function and cardiac output reserve, a key determinant of renal perfusion postoperatively. It is a strong, relatively unique baseline risk feature that can complement post-op hemodynamic variables in a sparse model.

Baseline RV function is closely tied to venous congestion and renal perfusion pressure (congestive nephropathy), especially after cardiac surgery. It provides unique predictive value beyond LVEF in older patients where RV failure/congestion is common.

Mitral regurgitation severity reflects chronic volume overload and may correlate with pulmonary pressures and perioperative complexity. It is a weaker, more indirect contributor and often mediated through LV/RV function measures already present.

Mitral stenosis indicates left atrial hypertension and potential pulmonary hypertension/RV strain. It can add some context but is relatively uncommon and its renal impact is largely captured by RV function and hemodynamic support variables.

Aortic regurgitation severity reflects chronic LV volume load and perioperative hemodynamic considerations. Its incremental value is usually limited once LVEF and other functional/hemodynamic features are available, making it a lower-priority sparse feature.

Pre-incision aortic valve disease severity can correlate with operative complexity and hemodynamic vulnerability. It is an indirect AKI risk factor and likely dominated by more proximal perfusion/vasopressor/renal markers, so low sparse-selection priority.
Indicator that aortic valve area was assessed (measurement/availability flag rather than physiology). Mostly a documentation/monitoring proxy with little unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Preoperative tricuspid regurgitation reflects right-sided dysfunction/venous congestion that can predispose to renal dysfunction. Still indirect and often redundant with postoperative hemodynamics/fluid balance signals, so modest priority.
Presence of a PFO is generally not mechanistically linked to postoperative AKI. Any association would be weak and confounded by procedure type, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Flag that the ascending aorta was assessed (process-of-care/TEE completeness). Provides minimal direct physiologic information and is unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Ascending aortic size relates to aortopathy and may influence surgical complexity/cross-clamp strategy. This is a distal, pre-op structural marker and typically weaker than direct perfusion/CPB and postoperative renal-perfusion features.

Aortic atheroma burden is a proxy for systemic atherosclerosis and embolic risk, which can contribute to renal injury. Compared with other pre-op echo descriptors here, thickness is a more quantitative severity measure and may add some unique signal.

Mobility suggests embolic potential but is a relatively subjective descriptor and less directly tied to renal hypoperfusion than CPB/fluids/hemodynamics. Likely redundant with other atheroma measures in this batch.
Whether the arch was visualized is primarily an imaging/assessment completeness variable. It is a clinician-behavior/technical proxy and not a direct renal-risk signal.

Maximum arch atheroma severity captures systemic atherosclerotic/embolic burden that can relate to postoperative organ dysfunction including AKI. It may carry more usable signal than mobility/visualization flags but remains indirect.

Arch atheroma mobility indicates embolic potential but overlaps heavily with arch atheroma severity (mx_arc_ath) and other atheroma descriptors. As a redundant, less quantitative feature, it is lower priority for a sparse model.

Use of cardiopulmonary bypass is a strong, well-established driver of AKI risk via inflammation, hemolysis, and nonpulsatile flow. It is a high-yield, nonredundant perioperative exposure likely to survive sparse selection.

Autologous priming/retrograde autologous priming reflects perfusion strategy affecting hemodilution and transfusion needs. It is an indirect practice-pattern variable and often dominated by direct fluid balance, transfusion, and CPB exposure measures.

Perfusion-team crystalloid volume reflects CPB prime/volume loading and hemodilution, which can influence renal perfusion and AKI risk. Useful but partially redundant with other volume/albumin/colloid totals and downstream fluid balance features.

Synthetic colloid exposure can affect renal function (agent-dependent) and indicates specific intraoperative volume strategy. Potentially informative and less common than crystalloid, but still a treatment-choice proxy and may be collinear with overall volume/CPB factors.

Albumin volume reflects oncotic/volume management during CPB and correlates with hemodilution and resuscitation intensity. Moderately predictive but likely shares signal with other perfusion fluids and overall fluid balance, limiting sparse-model uniqueness.

Use of hemofiltration/perfusion is a near-direct marker of severe fluid overload/AKI risk and renal-supportive intervention. It is a high-signal, relatively unique indicator likely to survive sparse selection for predicting later creatinine rise.

Need to wean inotropes off CPB reflects myocardial dysfunction and low-output risk that can drive renal hypoperfusion. Useful but indirect and often redundant with stronger hemodynamic/vasopressor or lactate signals.

Difficulty weaning vasopressors off CPB indicates vasoplegia/shock physiology closely tied to renal hypoperfusion and subsequent AKI. More directly linked to kidney risk than inotrope weaning and likely adds unique predictive value.

Having a post-TEE is largely a clinician-behavior/complexity marker rather than a physiologic renal signal. It may correlate with sicker cases but is unlikely to be retained in a sparse model over direct hemodynamic/lab features.

SAM suggests dynamic LVOT obstruction that can cause episodic hypotension. It is relatively rare and its renal impact is mediated through hemodynamics, making it a weaker, indirect sparse-model candidate.

Return-to-CPB/echo-related event flags intraoperative instability or technical issues. It is a coarse proxy for complexity and likely dominated by downstream shock markers (vasopressor need, lactate) for creatinine prediction.

Post-incision LVEF estimates baseline cardiac reserve and propensity for low cardiac output syndrome affecting renal perfusion. Moderately informative but indirect and potentially redundant with vasopressor/inotrope requirements and lactate.

Postoperative RV dysfunction can drive venous congestion and low forward flow, both relevant to AKI risk. Predictive but still a mediated signal and may be partially captured by pressor needs and lactate.

Intraoperative cardiac arrhythmias can cause transient hypoperfusion and hemodynamic instability. Moderately useful, but effects are often episodic and may be better summarized by lactate/pressor dependence.
Core temperature reflects rewarming/hypothermia and overall perioperative course but is not a direct renal biomarker. Likely a weak, nonspecific severity proxy for sparse selection.

INR reflects coagulopathy/hepatic dysfunction and bleeding risk, which can correlate with complexity and transfusion. It is indirect for AKI and typically dominated by hemodynamic and perfusion markers.
WBC is a nonspecific inflammatory/stress marker early post-op and often noisy. It provides little unique renal-specific signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.
Platelets can reflect CPB-related consumption, bleeding, or inflammation and correlate with illness severity. Indirect for renal injury and usually redundant with stronger perfusion/shock indicators.

Hematocrit captures anemia/hemodilution and bleeding/transfusion burden, which can affect renal oxygen delivery. Moderately useful but partly a proxy for fluid balance/transfusion and less direct than lactate/pressor signals.
Fibrinogen reflects inflammatory/consumptive coagulopathy and bleeding risk. It is an indirect severity marker and unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond hemodynamics and lactate.

Postoperative lactate is a strong marker of global hypoperfusion/low cardiac output and correlates with subsequent AKI. High-yield for sparse selection, though still a systemic (not kidney-specific) signal.

Likely reflects postoperative propofol exposure/sedation intensity, which is more a care-pattern/illness-severity proxy than a renal physiology marker. May correlate with hemodynamic instability but is indirect and unlikely to survive sparse selection versus direct perfusion/renal signals.

Pain score on postoperative day 3 is temporally misaligned with features collected only to 36h and is a weak, noisy proxy for recovery trajectory. It has little direct mechanistic link to AKI compared with hemodynamics, fluids, and renal labs.

Current smoking captures baseline vascular risk and pulmonary disease burden that can contribute to perioperative complications. However it is distal/indirect for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and often dominated by more proximal periop hemodynamics and renal markers.

Former smoking is an even weaker baseline risk proxy than current smoking and is heterogeneous in exposure/cessation time. Low unique predictive value for near-term postoperative creatinine changes in a sparse model.

Paroxysmal AF indicates baseline cardiac disease and potential for periop low-output episodes. Still indirect for AKI and likely redundant with stronger signals of perfusion/vasopressor need and heart failure severity.

Persistent AF more strongly reflects chronic atrial/structural heart disease and can associate with lower cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion risk. Moderately useful baseline context, but still secondary to direct postoperative hemodynamic/renal trajectory features.

Antegrade cardioplegia delivery is primarily a surgical technique variable and not a direct renal insult marker. Any association with AKI is likely confounded by procedure type/complexity and is weak for sparse selection.

Retrograde cardioplegia is another technique/procedure proxy; it may correlate with complex surgery but does not directly encode renal perfusion or injury. Likely redundant with better measures of operative complexity and postoperative physiology.

Blood cardioplegia type reflects operative preference and case mix rather than kidney-specific physiology. Expected to add minimal unique predictive value for creatinine ratio once other periop variables are included.
Crystalloid cardioplegia is similarly a procedural choice proxy with weak direct linkage to postoperative AKI. Low priority for a sparse linear model.

Acute heart failure timing suggests recent decompensation and higher risk of periop low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. Useful baseline risk stratifier, though still indirect compared with postoperative hemodynamics/urine output/renal labs.

Chronic heart failure indicates long-standing cardiorenal vulnerability and reduced renal reserve in very elderly patients. Moderately predictive baseline context but may be partially redundant with HF type and postoperative perfusion variables.

Systolic HF (reduced EF) is a strong baseline determinant of low-output risk and need for vasoactive support, both key drivers of AKI after cardiac surgery. High sparse-model priority as a relatively specific upstream cause of renal hypoperfusion.

Diastolic HF reflects impaired filling and sensitivity to volume shifts, which can contribute to congestion and renal dysfunction. Predictive but generally less directly tied to low-output AKI than systolic HF and may overlap with other comorbidity signals.

Mean SpO2 in the immediate 0–12h window is often influenced by ventilation/FiO2 settings and transient post-op factors, making it a noisy severity proxy. Mean aggregation further blunts brief hypoxemic insults and is unlikely to add unique value.

Mean SpO2 at 13–24h is somewhat more reflective of early trajectory than 0–12h and may capture ongoing respiratory failure that can worsen renal outcomes. Still indirect and mean-based (not capturing hypoxemia extremes), so modest sparse-model priority.

Represents average oxygen saturation in the closest window (25–36h). Mean SpO2 is a nonspecific illness/respiratory support marker and is usually dominated by hypoxemia extremes for AKI risk, so lower sparse-model priority.
Represents best (peak) oxygen saturation immediately post-op (0–12h). Max SpO2 is rarely informative for renal outcomes and is early/noisy, making it a weak sparse-selection candidate.
Represents peak oxygen saturation in 13–24h. Peak values mostly reflect supplemental oxygen/ventilation rather than renal perfusion risk and add little unique predictive value.
Represents peak oxygen saturation in 25–36h. Max SpO2 is typically ceilinged and treatment-dependent, offering minimal unique signal for creatinine rise compared with hypoxemia measures.

Represents worst hypoxemia in the immediate 0–12h period. Hypoxemia can correlate with shock/low output but early post-op artifacts and transient events reduce reliability for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Represents worst hypoxemia in 13–24h, which may reflect evolving cardiopulmonary failure and systemic hypoperfusion relevant to AKI. More informative than early window and than mean/max, but still indirect for renal injury.

Represents worst hypoxemia in the closest available window (25–36h). As an extreme, it better captures acute physiologic insult than mean/max and may add unique severity/perfusion information relevant to subsequent creatinine rise.

Counts/indicates frequency of SpO2 measurements in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and device sampling rather than physiology. Strongly proxy-like and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of SpO2 in 13–24h is primarily a clinician/monitoring proxy. Adds little unique renal predictive signal beyond actual saturation extremes.
Measurement frequency of SpO2 in 25–36h reflects monitoring/ICU workflow more than kidney risk. Even though temporally close, it is a weak proxy and low priority for a sparse model.

Represents average mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) early post-op, reflecting RV afterload/volume status. It is indirect for renal hypoperfusion and early values are noisy; likely redundant with later/heavier signals.

Represents sustained mPAP in 13–24h, which can indicate RV dysfunction/congestion affecting renal perfusion. Mean captures sustained load but is less sensitive than maxima and is still an indirect AKI predictor.

Represents sustained mPAP in the closest window (25–36h), potentially reflecting ongoing RV failure/venous congestion relevant to AKI. Useful but partially redundant with the corresponding max which better captures decompensation.
Represents peak mPAP in 0–12h, capturing acute RV afterload spikes. Early post-op hemodynamic volatility limits specificity for later creatinine rise, so modest priority.

Represents peak mPAP in 13–24h, which may capture episodes of RV strain/congestion linked to renal dysfunction. More informative than mean and earlier window, but still indirect and may correlate with other severity markers.

Represents peak mPAP in 25–36h, a near-term extreme that can reflect RV failure/venous congestion and impaired renal perfusion. Likely the most informative PA-pressure aggregation in this batch for sparse selection.

Minimum mean PAP in the immediate post-op window reflects early RV/pulmonary vascular load and congestion that can contribute to renal venous hypertension. However it is indirect for AKI and early (0–12h) values are noisy; later epochs would dominate in a sparse model.

Minimum mean PAP at 13–24h captures evolving pulmonary hypertension/RV strain that may relate to renal congestion. Still an indirect severity marker and likely redundant with CVP-based congestion signals, so low sparse-selection priority.

Minimum mean PAP at 25–36h is the closest window and may reflect persistent RV failure/pulmonary hypertension contributing to renal hypoperfusion/venous congestion. More temporally relevant than earlier epochs, but remains indirect and often overlaps with CVP information.

Count/frequency of PAP mean measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/illness-complexity proxy (e.g., PA catheter presence). It provides little direct physiologic signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of PAP mean in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and device use rather than renal pathophysiology. Redundant with other acuity proxies and low value for a sparse linear model.
Measurement frequency of PAP mean at 25–36h is still mainly a proxy for ongoing invasive monitoring and severity. Not a direct kidney-risk marker and should be deprioritized.

Frequency of continuous cardiac index recordings reflects device availability/monitoring intensity rather than actual perfusion status. As a proxy variable it is weak and dominated by true hemodynamic values if available.
Measurement frequency of continuous cardiac index is largely clinician/device-driven and not a direct renal insult marker. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.
Even in the closest window, cardiac index measurement frequency is a monitoring proxy rather than physiology. Low priority compared with actual hemodynamic extremes/levels.

Mean CVP in 0–12h reflects venous congestion/right-sided filling pressures that can impair renal function. Early post-op fluid shifts and ventilation effects make it noisier and less predictive than later CVP summaries.

Mean CVP at 13–24h captures sustained venous congestion, a plausible contributor to AKI via renal venous hypertension. Useful but somewhat redundant with CVP max (which better captures harmful extremes).

Mean CVP at 25–36h is temporally closest and reflects ongoing congestion/volume overload, a strong mechanistic driver of postoperative AKI in cardiac surgery. Likely to retain unique predictive value in sparse selection, though still related to CVP max.
Peak CVP in 0–12h captures episodes of marked venous congestion that can precipitate renal dysfunction. However early post-op peaks can be transient/ventilator-related, so later peaks are preferred.

Peak CVP at 13–24h captures clinically important congestion extremes after initial stabilization, which are strongly linked to AKI risk. High sparse-model priority as an extreme aggregation with clearer pathophysiologic signal than the mean.

Peak CVP at 25–36h is the closest and best captures severe ongoing venous congestion/right heart failure, a high-yield predictor of subsequent creatinine rise. Among this batch it is the strongest, least replaceable renal-relevant hemodynamic signal.

Minimum CVP in 0–12h may reflect hypovolemia or measurement/PEEP effects but is less directly tied to AKI than congestion metrics. It is early, noisy, and dominated by CVP max/mean for renal prediction.

Minimum CVP in 13–24h reflects lowest venous congestion/preload and can relate to renal perfusion/venous outflow. Moderately useful but likely weaker than later-window CVP and can be redundant with other hemodynamic/volume-status signals.

Minimum CVP in 25–36h is a near-term marker of venous congestion/underfilling that can influence renal perfusion and AKI risk. Closest window and an extreme summary makes it a strong sparse-model candidate within the CVP family.

Count/frequency of CVP measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/line-management proxy rather than physiology. Early post-op period is noisy and this is unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Frequency of CVP measurement in 13–24h reflects clinician concern/ICU complexity more than renal pathophysiology. Typically dominated by actual CVP values and other severity markers, so low priority for sparse selection.

Frequency of CVP measurement in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability or fluid/pressor titration near the prediction horizon. Still largely a behavior/monitoring signal and usually redundant with hemodynamic extremes.

Mean FiO2 in 0–12h reflects early ventilatory support/oxygenation strategy immediately post-op. It is an indirect severity proxy and early values are often protocol-driven, so low unique value for predicting creatinine ratio.

Mean FiO2 in 13–24h captures sustained oxygen requirement and overall cardiopulmonary severity. Indirectly related to AKI risk but generally redundant with stronger hemodynamic/renal markers, so only weak-to-moderate priority.

Mean FiO2 in 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and reflects ongoing respiratory/circulatory illness burden. Still an indirect proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection if direct renal/perfusion features are available.
Maximum FiO2 in 0–12h often reflects transient post-bypass/anesthesia effects or brief events. Early extremes are noisy and not kidney-specific, so minimal sparse-model value.
Maximum FiO2 in 13–24h may capture acute respiratory decompensation and higher illness severity. It can add some signal beyond the mean but remains indirect for renal outcomes and likely replaceable.

Maximum FiO2 in 25–36h captures late acute oxygenation/ventilatory escalation near the prediction horizon, a marker of significant physiologic stress. Among FiO2 summaries, this extreme in the latest window is most likely to add unique sparse-model signal.
Minimum FiO2 in 0–12h largely reflects baseline ventilator settings and early weaning/protocols. It is weakly related to renal dysfunction and dominated by later-window severity indicators.

Minimum FiO2 in 13–24h mainly indicates ability to wean oxygen support, which is an indirect and often protocol-driven signal. Typically less informative than max/mean FiO2 for capturing acute stress relevant to AKI risk.

Minimum FiO2 in 25–36h reflects the best oxygen requirement achieved late, which can indicate recovery vs persistent illness. Some proximity benefit, but still indirect and usually weaker than late FiO2 max for capturing decompensation.

FiO2 measurement frequency in 0–12h is a documentation/monitoring proxy and often near-constant on ventilated patients. Adds little unique physiologic information for sparse prediction of creatinine ratio.

FiO2 measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects charting intensity and ventilator management rather than oxygenation physiology. Generally redundant with FiO2 values themselves and not a strong sparse-model feature.

Count/frequency of FiO2 documentation in 25–36h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and ventilator management. This is a clinician-behavior proxy with little direct renal signal and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Average set ventilator respiratory rate in the immediate 0–12h post-op window. Largely reflects ventilation strategy/anesthesia effects and is an indirect severity marker with high noise and redundancy vs later windows/extremes.

Average set respiratory rate during 13–24h, reflecting ongoing ventilatory support needs. Still indirect for AKI and likely redundant with RR extremes and other severity/hemodynamic variables, but more informative than 0–12h.

Peak set respiratory rate in 0–12h, which may capture early respiratory distress/acidosis management. Extremes are better than means, but this early window is noisy and the signal is indirect for creatinine rise.

Peak set respiratory rate in 13–24h, a better marker of sustained respiratory failure/metabolic acidosis compensation than the mean. Still an indirect illness-severity proxy, but the extreme in this mid window is the most likely RR-derived feature to add unique value.
Lowest set respiratory rate in 0–12h, often reflecting sedation/weaning rather than organ hypoperfusion. Weak and noisy for renal prediction and dominated by later-window severity indicators.

Lowest set respiratory rate in 13–24h, typically reflecting ventilator weaning/sedation depth. Provides little direct AKI information and is redundant with more informative extremes (e.g., RR max) if any RR feature is kept.
Frequency of recorded set respiratory rate in 0–12h, a documentation/monitoring proxy. Not a physiologic biomarker and unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Frequency of recorded set respiratory rate in 13–24h, reflecting charting intensity and care complexity. Generally weak for sparse modeling because it is indirect and confounded by workflow.

Frequency of recorded set respiratory rate in 25–36h, again primarily a monitoring/ventilation-management proxy. Despite being the closest window, it remains non-physiologic and low-yield for AKI prediction.

Average set tidal volume in 0–12h, largely determined by protocol (e.g., lung-protective ventilation) and body size. Early post-op values are noisy and provide minimal unique renal prognostic information.

Average set tidal volume in 13–24h, reflecting ongoing ventilator strategy and possibly lung mechanics. Indirect for kidney outcomes and often protocol-driven, but may weakly track severity beyond the immediate post-op period.

Average set tidal volume in 25–36h, the closest window and may reflect persistent respiratory failure vs weaning. Still largely clinician-set/protocolized and indirect for AKI, so only a weak-to-moderate sparse-model candidate.
Maximum set tidal volume in 0–12h, often reflecting initial ventilator setup rather than evolving physiology. Early and protocol-driven, offering little unique signal for later creatinine ratio.

Maximum set tidal volume in 13–24h, potentially capturing deviations from lung-protective settings due to mechanics or acidosis. Still indirect and likely redundant with mean VT and other severity markers, but the extreme is the better VT aggregation.

Maximum set tidal volume in 25–36h, which may capture persistent high ventilatory demand or poor lung mechanics closest to the prediction horizon. Among VT features, this extreme in the latest window is most likely to add incremental severity information, though still indirect for renal injury.

Minimum set tidal volume in the immediate post-op window mainly reflects ventilator strategy/body size rather than renal physiology. Likely weak and indirect for AKI prediction and dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers in a sparse model.

Minimum set tidal volume from 13–24h is still primarily a ventilation management choice and correlates more with lung mechanics than kidney injury. Adds little unique value for creatinine_ratio compared with perfusion/renal signals.

Minimum set tidal volume at 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing respiratory failure severity and sedation/vent dependence closer to the outcome. Still indirect and likely not selected unless few other severity variables exist.

Count/frequency of tidal volume setting measurements is a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than a physiologic biomarker. Early post-op measurement frequency is especially noisy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of tidal volume settings reflects clinician attention/ventilator adjustments more than kidney risk. Provides minimal unique predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Later measurement frequency can proxy ongoing instability/vent management complexity closer to Hour 60. Still a behavior/monitoring surrogate and typically low priority in a sparse linear model.

Mean PEEP in 0–12h reflects early respiratory support and can influence venous return/renal perfusion, but this window is confounded by immediate post-bypass physiology. Mean also smooths extremes, so it is a weaker renal-insult signal.

Mean PEEP at 13–24h may indicate sustained higher intrathoracic pressure and illness severity with potential renal perfusion effects. However it remains indirect and is likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/vasopressor features.

Mean PEEP at 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may capture sustained high PEEP that can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion. Among PEEP summaries, this is a more plausible keeper if ventilatory support is a key severity axis.

Maximum PEEP in 0–12h captures extreme ventilatory support that could transiently impair hemodynamics, but early post-op extremes are often procedural/noise-driven. Indirect for AKI and likely dominated by later windows.

Maximum PEEP at 13–24h captures episodes of high intrathoracic pressure and severe lung dysfunction that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. As an extreme, it is more informative than mean/min but still an indirect severity proxy.

Maximum PEEP at 25–36h is the closest extreme ventilatory-support signal and best captures acute high-PEEP exposure that can depress preload/cardiac output and worsen renal perfusion. Within this batch, it is the strongest PEEP-derived candidate for sparse selection.

Minimum PEEP in the immediate post-op period mostly reflects baseline ventilator settings and offers little information about renal risk. It is dominated by max/mean PEEP measures and by later epochs.
Minimum PEEP at 13–24h is typically near routine settings and does not capture the injurious/hemodynamically relevant exposure. Redundant with more informative max/mean PEEP features.

Minimum PEEP at 25–36h may weakly indicate ability to wean ventilatory support (overall recovery), which can correlate with lower AKI risk. Still indirect and generally less informative than max PEEP for capturing insults.

PEEP measurement frequency is primarily a monitoring/adjustment intensity proxy rather than a physiologic renal predictor. Early post-op measurement patterns are especially confounded and low priority for sparse modeling.

Count/frequency of PEEP documentation in hours 13–24, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and ventilator management. Weak, indirect proxy for severity and unlikely to add unique renal predictive value in a sparse model.

Frequency of PEEP measurements in hours 25–36, a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy rather than a physiologic renal signal. Slightly higher only because it is closer to Hour 60 and may correlate with ongoing respiratory failure severity.

Average plateau pressure in the immediate 0–12h post-op window, reflecting ventilator mechanics and lung compliance. Early means are noisy and indirect for AKI risk, and dominated by later/peak severity signals.

Mean plateau pressure in hours 13–24, a marker of sustained ventilatory stress/poor compliance. Still indirect for kidney injury and likely redundant with more informative extremes (max) and later windows.

Peak plateau pressure in 0–12h, capturing transient high intrathoracic pressures that can reduce venous return/renal perfusion. However, early post-op peaks are often procedural/anesthesia-related and less predictive of Hour 60 creatinine than later trajectory.

Peak plateau pressure in 13–24h, reflecting more persistent lung dysfunction and higher intrathoracic pressures that can impair renal perfusion. Among plateau summaries, the max is the most informative aggregation, though still an indirect AKI predictor.
Minimum plateau pressure in 0–12h, largely reflecting periods of low support or measurement conditions rather than injury. Low unique value and dominated by max/trajectory measures for severity.

Minimum plateau pressure in 13–24h, not a clear pathophysiologic driver of AKI and often reflects ventilator settings rather than organ dysfunction. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given redundancy with max/mean.
Frequency of plateau pressure measurements in 0–12h, primarily a monitoring/RT workflow proxy. Very indirect and early post-op measurement patterns are noisy for predicting Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Count of plateau pressure measurements in 13–24h, reflecting clinician attention/ventilator management rather than physiology. Low priority for sparse modeling due to proxy nature and redundancy with actual pressure values.

Frequency of plateau pressure measurements in 25–36h, a proxy for ongoing ventilatory complexity closer to the outcome time. Still indirect, but may weakly add information about persistent critical illness when few other severity markers are present.

Average minute ventilation in 0–12h, influenced by sedation, temperature, and immediate post-bypass physiology. Early mean values are noisy and indirect for AKI, and likely redundant with later/peak respiratory severity measures.

Mean minute ventilation in 13–24h, reflecting sustained ventilatory demand (metabolic acidosis, dead space, or agitation). Indirect for renal dysfunction and typically weaker than extremes or direct hemodynamic/renal markers.

Peak minute ventilation in 0–12h, which can signal early acidosis/CO2 burden but is often confounded by immediate post-op management. Some potential severity signal, but timing and confounding limit sparse-model priority.

Peak minute ventilation in 13–24h, a better marker of evolving metabolic/respiratory stress (e.g., acidosis) that can accompany hypoperfusion and AKI risk. Among minute ventilation summaries, the max is most likely to add unique predictive value.
Minimum minute ventilation in 0–12h, commonly driven by sedation/ventilator strategy rather than organ dysfunction. Low interpretability for AKI prediction and dominated by max/trajectory measures.

Minimum minute ventilation reflects the lowest ventilatory support/drive (often tied to sedation or weaning) rather than a direct renal signal. It is an indirect severity proxy and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/renal predictors, so low sparse-model priority.

Count/frequency of minute ventilation documentation is mainly a monitoring-intensity/ventilation-management proxy. Early (0–12h) measurement frequency is especially noisy post-op and offers little unique value for creatinine ratio.

Measurement frequency of minute ventilation reflects clinician attention and ventilator management rather than kidney physiology. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given indirectness and redundancy with actual physiologic variables.
Later measurement frequency may weakly proxy ongoing respiratory failure/ICU complexity closer to the outcome window. Still primarily a behavior/monitoring signal, so only modest sparse-model value.

Average oxygen device level in the immediate post-op window reflects routine extubation/oxygen escalation patterns more than renal perfusion. Mean aggregation and early timing make it noisy and dominated by later/extreme measures.

Mean oxygen device requirement over 13–24h is a coarse marker of respiratory support and overall illness severity. It is indirect for AKI and likely redundant with a max (escalation) measure and later window data.

Sustained oxygen device requirement closer to Hour 60 can reflect persistent cardiopulmonary dysfunction that correlates with renal hypoperfusion/congestion. Mean is still less informative than extremes, but timing makes it a moderate sparse candidate within this batch.
