Maximum oxygen device in 0–12h captures early escalation (e.g., delayed extubation/high support) but this period is confounded by anesthesia and routine post-bypass physiology. Useful as a weak severity proxy but not a strong unique renal predictor.

Peak oxygen device requirement in 13–24h better captures clinically meaningful respiratory deterioration than the mean and may track low cardiac output/pulmonary edema linked to AKI risk. Still indirect and potentially redundant with 25–36h status.

Peak oxygen device requirement in 25–36h is the closest and most extreme summary of ongoing respiratory/cardiac dysfunction, which often co-travels with renal hypoperfusion or congestion. Within this batch, this extreme late-window feature is the strongest sparse-model candidate.

Minimum oxygen device in 0–12h largely reflects immediate post-extubation baseline support and documentation practices. It is not a meaningful renal-risk signal and is dominated by max/late-window measures.
Minimum oxygen device over 13–24h mainly indicates best-case support level rather than episodes of deterioration. It adds little unique predictive value compared with max/mean measures.

Minimum oxygen device in 25–36h reflects the best respiratory status achieved in the closest window, which may weakly indicate recovery trajectory. However it is less informative than peak support needs and thus lower sparse priority.

Frequency of oxygen device documentation is a monitoring/RT workflow proxy, not a physiologic biomarker. Early post-op measurement intensity is especially non-specific for predicting creatinine ratio.
Measurement frequency of oxygen device reflects care processes and perceived instability rather than kidney injury mechanisms. Unlikely to add unique value in a sparse linear model.
Later measurement frequency may weakly proxy ongoing respiratory support changes and ICU complexity closer to the outcome. Still primarily a clinician-behavior signal, so only modest importance.

Average early chest tube output reflects postoperative bleeding/volume loss and can relate indirectly to renal hypoperfusion. Mean in the immediate 0–12h window is noisy and largely redundant with later-window or extreme-output summaries for sparse selection.

Mean chest tube output in 13–24h captures ongoing bleeding/drainage burden after initial stabilization. Still an indirect AKI signal and likely dominated by max output and the closer 25–36h window if choosing sparsely.

Peak chest tube output in 25–36h captures late/ongoing bleeding or tamponade-related physiology close to the outcome window, which can strongly affect renal perfusion and transfusion exposure. Average sinus rhythm presence in 13–24h, capturing sustained rhythm stability. Still an indirect proxy for perfusion/acuity and likely dominated by more direct hemodynamic/vasopressor features (not in this batch).

Average sinus rhythm presence closest to the prediction horizon, reflecting ongoing rhythm stability that can affect renal perfusion. Among rhythm summaries, this timing is most relevant and may add modest unique value.
Whether sinus rhythm occurred at any point in 0–12h (a very weak/ceiling-prone indicator). Highly redundant with mean/min and provides little discrimination for AKI risk.

Whether sinus rhythm occurred at any point in 13–24h. Max is typically uninformative because most patients will have at least some sinus rhythm, making it redundant and low-yield for sparse selection.
Whether sinus rhythm occurred at any point in 25–36h. Despite better timing, the max aggregation is still a low-information indicator and dominated by mean/min rhythm measures.

Whether the patient was continuously in sinus rhythm throughout 0–12h (min=1 implies no arrhythmia). More informative than max, but early post-op rhythm instability is common and may not uniquely predict 60h creatinine.
Continuous sinus rhythm during 13–24h, capturing sustained absence of arrhythmia that can impact perfusion. This extreme (min) is more discriminative than max and may add moderate unique signal.

Continuous sinus rhythm during 25–36h, the closest window to Hour 60, reflecting sustained rhythm stability vs ongoing arrhythmia burden. Among rhythm features, this extreme and timing best preserve a potentially renal-relevant hemodynamic signal and is the top sparse-model candidate in this batch.

Count/frequency of documentation of normal sinus rhythm in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring/documentation intensity proxy. It has little direct renal signal and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of normal sinus rhythm in 13–24h reflects charting/monitoring rather than physiology. Weak and indirect for AKI prediction and dominated by hemodynamic/renal markers not in this batch.

Later (25–36h) monitoring frequency may weakly proxy ongoing instability/ICU complexity closer to the outcome window. Still largely clinician-behavior driven and not a direct kidney perfusion/injury marker.

Average burden of sinus rhythm variants in 0–12h is early post-op noise and not tightly linked to renal injury. Mean aggregation further dilutes brief clinically relevant episodes, making it low priority.

Mean sinus-variant burden in 13–24h may reflect evolving conduction/atrial irritability but is an indirect renal risk signal. Likely redundant with a simpler presence/absence extreme and with arrhythmia indicators.

Sustained sinus-variant burden closer to Hour 60 could weakly correlate with ongoing illness/hemodynamic lability. However it remains indirect for AKI and mean aggregation is typically dominated by a max/presence feature.

Any occurrence of sinus rhythm variant in the first 12h captures an extreme/presence signal better than the mean. Still early and nonspecific for renal hypoperfusion/injury, so low-to-moderate sparse value.

Presence of sinus rhythm variant in 13–24h may indicate ongoing conduction instability and potential hemodynamic variability. As an extreme it is the best version within this family, but remains an indirect AKI predictor.

Presence of sinus rhythm variant in 25–36h is the closest window and the most informative aggregation (max) for capturing clinically meaningful episodes. Still a proxy for instability rather than a direct renal marker, so not top-tier.

Minimum of a binary sinus-variant indicator is largely uninformative (often just indicates it was not continuously present). It adds little beyond max/mean and is especially noisy in the immediate post-op window.
Binary min is a weak aggregation that is typically redundant with max and provides little incremental information. Not a plausible sparse-model keeper for predicting creatinine trajectory.
Even in the closest window, binary min conveys little clinically actionable signal compared with max (any occurrence). Highly redundant and unlikely to survive feature selection.
Frequency of measuring/documenting sinus variants in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring intensity proxy. It is indirect and dominated by actual rhythm burden (e.g., max) if any rhythm feature is kept.
Measurement count in 13–24h reflects clinician attention and telemetry documentation rather than physiology. Low unique predictive value for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
Later measurement frequency could weakly proxy ongoing instability closer to the outcome time. Still largely behavior-driven and redundant with rhythm-burden extremes, so only a weak candidate.

Mean burden of atrial arrhythmia (e.g., AF/flutter) in 0–12h can relate to hemodynamic compromise and need for vasoactive support, which can affect renal perfusion. Despite early timing and mean dilution, it is more clinically consequential than sinus variants and may add unique signal within this batch.

Represents proportion of time in atrial arrhythmia during 13–24h, a marker of hemodynamic instability/illness severity. Likely redundant with simpler presence/absence (max) and less informative than the later 25–36h window for sparse selection.

Represents burden of atrial arrhythmia in the 25–36h window, which can contribute to reduced cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion. More temporally relevant than earlier windows, but still an indirect AKI predictor and often dominated by hypotension/vasopressor/urine-output signals not shown here.

Binary indicator of any atrial arrhythmia occurrence in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Early post-bypass rhythm issues are common/transient and less predictive of Hour-60 creatinine than later persistence, so lower sparse-model priority.

Binary indicator of any atrial arrhythmia during 13–24h, reflecting early postoperative instability that may affect renal perfusion. Useful and simpler than mean, but still indirect and somewhat redundant with the 25–36h version.

Binary indicator of any atrial arrhythmia in the 25–36h window, closest to the outcome and capturing persistent/new instability. Among atrial-arrhythmia features, this is the most sparse-model-friendly (simple, temporally proximate) and likely to add unique signal.

Minimum of a binary atrial-arrhythmia indicator over 0–12h effectively reflects whether arrhythmia was absent at any time, which is not clinically meaningful. Dominated by the corresponding max (any occurrence) and adds little unique predictive value.
Minimum of a binary atrial-arrhythmia indicator over 13–24h is largely uninformative (often 0 unless arrhythmia is continuous). Redundant with max/mean and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Minimum of a binary atrial-arrhythmia indicator over 25–36h mainly captures continuous arrhythmia vs intermittent, but is a weak/awkward encoding. Typically dominated by max (presence) and mean (burden) for predictive utility.
Count/frequency of atrial-arrhythmia measurements in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring-intensity/EMR artifact proxy. Not a direct physiologic renal signal and likely noisy early post-op.

Measurement frequency for atrial arrhythmia in 13–24h reflects clinician attention/telemetry documentation rather than arrhythmia burden itself. Low unique value for sparse prediction compared with max/mean indicators.

Measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability prompting closer monitoring. Still largely a behavior/complexity proxy and inferior to direct arrhythmia presence/burden for sparse selection.

Represents proportion of time paced in 0–12h, often reflecting conduction disease or post-op support needs. Early pacing is common and may be transient; it is an indirect severity marker with limited unique renal predictive value.
Represents pacing burden in 13–24h, a proxy for ongoing conduction issues/low-output management. Indirect for AKI and likely redundant with hemodynamic variables; modest sparse-model priority.

Represents pacing burden in 25–36h, which may indicate persistent low-output state or need for chronotropic support affecting renal perfusion. Best-timed paced-rhythm summary here, but still a proxy and not a direct kidney marker.
Binary indicator of any pacing in 0–12h, capturing immediate post-op pacing use. Early pacing is frequent and nonspecific; adds limited unique information beyond later persistence/burden.

Binary indicator of any pacing in 13–24h, reflecting continued need for pacing support. Simpler than mean but still an indirect severity proxy and likely dominated by the later 25–36h pacing burden if included.

Indicates any paced rhythm occurrence in the 25–36h window, a proxy for conduction disease/low-output risk. Weak and indirect for AKI prediction and largely redundant with paced_rhythm_min in the same epoch.
Early (0–12h) paced rhythm presence is common post-op and often transient. Low unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and dominated by later-epoch rhythm status.
Paced rhythm in 13–24h may reflect persistent conduction issues and possible hemodynamic compromise. Still an indirect renal signal and likely redundant with the closer 25–36h epoch.

Paced rhythm in the closest window (25–36h) suggests ongoing conduction abnormality and potential low cardiac output affecting renal perfusion. Moderate sparse-model value as a near-term hemodynamic proxy, but still indirect compared with direct renal/hemodynamic features.

Measurement count/frequency of paced rhythm documentation in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and charting patterns. Poor direct physiologic content and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Frequency of paced rhythm measurement in 13–24h is primarily a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy. Adds little beyond the binary rhythm indicators and is not a direct AKI precursor.
Measurement frequency in 25–36h reflects surveillance/complexity rather than renal physiology. Redundant with paced rhythm presence and low priority for a sparse linear model.

Average heart block burden in 0–12h is often influenced by immediate post-op transient conduction changes. Mean aggregation dilutes episodic events and is weak/indirect for predicting creatinine rise at 60h.

Sustained heart block burden in 13–24h may correlate with hemodynamic instability and renal hypoperfusion risk. Still indirect and likely dominated by a simpler extreme/occurrence indicator in the closer epoch.
Mean heart block burden in 25–36h captures persistence but smooths clinically important episodes. Moderate-to-low sparse value as an indirect low-output proxy, with binary max/min likely sufficient.
Any heart block occurrence in 0–12h is common and may be transient after surgery. Early timing and indirect linkage to AKI make it low priority.
Any heart block in 13–24h suggests more persistent conduction disturbance and potential low cardiac output. Still an indirect renal predictor and less valuable than the closest 25–36h occurrence.

Any heart block in 25–36h indicates ongoing conduction abnormality near the prediction horizon, plausibly affecting renal perfusion. Reasonable sparse-model candidate within this batch, though still a proxy rather than a direct renal marker.
Heart block presence in 0–12h (min as binary) is early and often transient post-op. Redundant with other heart block indicators and weak for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Heart block presence in 13–24h may reflect clinically meaningful conduction issues. Indirect for AKI and largely redundant with the 25–36h heart block occurrence.

Heart block presence in 25–36h is the most temporally relevant indicator of persistent conduction disease and possible low-output physiology. Moderate priority in a sparse model, but still secondary to direct renal/hemodynamic variables not in this batch.

Count/frequency of documenting heart block in the first 12h is mainly a monitoring/annotation intensity proxy. It is indirect for AKI risk and early post-op rhythm issues are often transient, so low sparse-model priority.

Measurement frequency of heart block in 13–24h may reflect persistent conduction issues and higher acuity. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy rather than a physiologic renal signal, so only weak incremental value.

Later (25–36h) documentation frequency could correlate with ongoing low-output states that predispose to AKI. However it remains a measured/proxy feature and is likely dominated by direct hemodynamics/renal markers, so modest score.

Average presence of ischemic ST changes in 0–12h suggests perioperative myocardial ischemia. It is an indirect AKI risk marker and the mean in the immediate post-op window is noisy and redundant with more informative extremes/later windows.

Mean ischemic ST change in 13–24h may indicate sustained ischemia/low cardiac output, which can drive renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect and likely redundant with a max indicator, but could add some trajectory information.

Mean ischemic ST change in 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and may reflect ongoing myocardial ischemia affecting renal perfusion. However, mean is typically dominated by an extreme (max) indicator for sparse selection.
Any ischemic ST change in 0–12h captures an acute ischemic insult that could precede low-output AKI. Early timing is less reliable post-bypass, but the max is the best aggregation within this epoch.

Any ischemic ST change in 13–24h is a strong marker of clinically meaningful myocardial ischemia and potential low cardiac output, a key upstream driver of AKI. As an extreme indicator it is less redundant than means and likely to survive sparse screening within this batch.

Any ischemic ST change in 25–36h is the closest window and best captures ongoing ischemia that can impair renal perfusion leading to higher creatinine at 60h. High priority among these rhythm-derived features, though still indirect vs direct renal labs.
Minimum of a binary ischemic ST-change flag is typically uninformative (often just indicates at least one non-ischemic reading). It adds little beyond max/mean and is poor for sparse selection.
Minimum of a binary ST-change indicator is largely redundant and does not capture the insult (max does). Very low incremental predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Minimum of a binary ST-change flag remains non-informative and dominated by the max indicator in the same window. Not a candidate for a sparse model.
Frequency of recording ST-change in 0–12h reflects monitoring intensity and documentation practices. It is not a direct physiologic signal and is especially noisy early post-op.
More frequent ST-change measurements in 13–24h may indicate clinician concern or instability. Still a proxy feature and likely redundant with actual ST-change presence (max), so low priority.

Measurement frequency in 25–36h could track ongoing instability/concern closer to the outcome. Nonetheless it is a monitoring proxy and typically dominated by physiologic/hemodynamic variables, so modest score.

Average burden of 'other' rhythm category in 0–12h is a nonspecific arrhythmia signal that may correlate with illness severity. It is poorly specific, early/noisy, and likely redundant with clearer hemodynamic/vasopressor features for AKI prediction.

Represents proportion of time in a non-standard/other rhythm in hours 13–24. Rhythm category is an indirect severity/hemodynamic proxy and the mean is likely redundant with closer-window indicators, so low sparse-model priority.

Represents proportion of time in a non-standard/other rhythm in hours 25–36. Closer timing helps, but this remains a nonspecific proxy for instability and is unlikely to add unique renal signal versus direct perfusion/vasopressor/urine output features.

Binary indicator that an 'other' rhythm occurred at any point in hours 0–12. Early post-op rhythm abnormalities are often transient/noisy and this is a weak, indirect predictor of later creatinine rise.

Binary indicator that an 'other' rhythm occurred at any point in hours 13–24. Captures an extreme (any occurrence) but remains nonspecific and likely redundant with more direct hemodynamic/vasopressor signals.

Binary indicator that an 'other' rhythm occurred at any point in hours 25–36. The late window and extreme encoding may reflect ongoing instability that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion, giving modest unique value within this batch.

Binary indicator for minimum presence of 'other' rhythm in hours 0–12 (typically redundant with max/mean for a binary rhythm flag). Early timing and redundancy make it very unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Binary indicator for minimum presence of 'other' rhythm in hours 13–24, which is generally uninformative/redundant for binary rhythm occurrence. Adds little beyond the corresponding max and is an indirect renal predictor.

Binary indicator for minimum presence of 'other' rhythm in hours 25–36, typically redundant and not clinically interpretable as an extreme insult. Low incremental value versus the max indicator in the same window.
Count/frequency of documenting 'other' rhythm in hours 0–12. This is primarily monitoring/annotation intensity rather than physiology, and early post-op measurement patterns are especially noisy.
Count/frequency of documenting 'other' rhythm in hours 13–24. Mostly a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy and unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.

Count/frequency of documenting 'other' rhythm in hours 25–36. Still largely a monitoring-intensity proxy, but later increased attention may weakly track ongoing instability, so slightly higher than earlier windows.

Represents proportion of time on ASV ventilator mode in hours 0–12. Vent mode choice is largely practice/patient-management driven and early post-op ventilation is routine, offering little direct renal signal.

Represents proportion of time on ASV ventilator mode in hours 13–24. May weakly reflect respiratory trajectory/sedation needs but is an indirect, practice-dependent proxy and not a strong sparse-model keeper for creatinine ratio.
Binary indicator that ASV mode was used at any point in hours 0–12. Early ventilator mode exposure is common and heavily clinician-driven, so minimal unique predictive value for AKI.

Binary indicator that ASV mode was used at any point in hours 13–24. Slightly more informative than 0–12h as it may reflect delayed extubation/complexity, but remains indirect and likely dominated by direct hemodynamic/renal features.
Binary indicator for minimum ASV presence in hours 0–12 (generally redundant/unhelpful for a binary mode flag). Adds no meaningful extreme physiology signal and is unlikely to be selected sparsely.

Whether ASV mode was used at all in 13–24h is mainly a ventilation-management choice and weakly related to renal physiology. Likely redundant with broader severity/ventilation duration signals and unlikely to survive sparse selection for creatinine ratio.

Count/frequency of ASV documentation in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring/documentation proxy rather than a physiologic renal-risk marker. Early post-op window adds noise and this is unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Measurement frequency of ASV in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior and charting intensity more than kidney injury risk. In a sparse model it is dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal features (not present in this batch) and by simpler “any ASV” indicators.

ASV measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing respiratory support/illness severity closer to the 60h outcome. Still largely a documentation/management proxy and not a direct renal signal, so low priority.

Average use/intensity of controlled ventilation in 0–12h reflects immediate post-op ventilatory support and sedation needs. Early timing and indirectness to renal hypoperfusion make it a weak, replaceable severity proxy.

Mean controlled ventilation in 13–24h suggests ongoing need for full support, which can correlate with overall acuity. However it is indirect for AKI and likely redundant with simpler binary indicators (max/any) and other severity variables.

Any controlled ventilation in 0–12h is common after cardiac surgery and often reflects routine immediate post-op management. As an early, nonspecific severity marker it has limited unique value for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.

Presence of controlled ventilation in 13–24h indicates failure to wean and higher illness severity, which can track with AKI risk. Among controlled-mode summaries, this “any use” extreme is more informative than means, but remains indirect.

A minimum indicator for controlled mode in 0–12h is hard to interpret clinically and is typically redundant with max/any-use flags. Early post-op period further reduces its usefulness for sparse selection.
Minimum controlled-mode indicator in 13–24h is an awkward aggregation and provides little beyond max/any-use measures. Likely redundant and not a direct renal-risk signal.
Frequency of controlled-mode documentation in 0–12h is mainly a charting/monitoring proxy. It is early and indirect, so very unlikely to be retained in a sparse model for creatinine ratio.

Controlled-mode measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects documentation intensity and ventilator management rather than kidney physiology. Redundant with simpler controlled-mode presence indicators and low priority for sparse selection.

Controlled-mode measurement frequency in 25–36h may proxy persistent mechanical ventilation closer to the outcome window. Still largely clinician-behavior/management driven and weaker than a simple “any controlled ventilation” indicator.
Mean SIMV use in 0–12h is a ventilation strategy detail during a noisy immediate post-op period. It is an indirect severity proxy and unlikely to add unique predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio.

Mean SIMV use in 13–24h may reflect partial support and weaning trajectory, loosely tied to overall recovery. However it remains indirect for AKI and likely redundant with broader ventilation duration/severity signals.
Any SIMV use in 0–12h is common and largely reflects routine ventilator management rather than renal insult. Early timing and nonspecificity make it a poor sparse-model candidate.

SIMV use in 13–24h reflects ongoing ventilatory support/sedation and overall illness severity. Weak, indirect link to AKI and largely redundant with other ventilation-mode indicators, so low sparse-model priority.
Whether SIMV was ever absent/present early (0–12h) is dominated by immediate post-op practice patterns and transient instability. Very indirect for renal outcome and redundant with later windows.
Minimum SIMV indicator in 13–24h captures whether the patient ever transitioned off SIMV in that window. Still mostly a care-pattern/respiratory trajectory proxy with limited unique renal signal.

Count/frequency of SIMV documentation in 0–12h is primarily monitoring/documentation intensity rather than physiology. Poor direct relevance to creatinine trajectory and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of SIMV in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior/EMR density proxy. Adds little unique predictive value for AKI compared with hemodynamics/labs.
Later (25–36h) documentation frequency may weakly reflect persistent critical illness closer to the 60h target. Still a monitoring-intensity proxy and not a direct renal signal, so only modest value.
Average presence of spontaneous mode in 0–12h mainly reflects early extubation practices and immediate post-op course. Early window is noisy and this is indirect for kidney injury.

Spontaneous mode in 13–24h suggests improving respiratory status and less sedation/pressors, indirectly associated with lower AKI risk. However it is still a nonspecific severity proxy and redundant with max/min indicators.
Any spontaneous mode in 0–12h is heavily influenced by early extubation protocols and transient post-op factors. Weak and indirect for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.

Any spontaneous mode in 13–24h captures whether the patient could tolerate lighter support, a general recovery marker. Indirect renal relevance and likely redundant with other ventilation-mode features.

Minimum spontaneous indicator early is essentially the complement of being continuously spontaneous and is dominated by routine immediate post-op ventilation. Minimal unique information for AKI prediction.
Whether the patient was continuously spontaneous through 13–24h reflects a smoother recovery trajectory. Still indirect and likely outcompeted by direct perfusion/renal markers in a sparse model.
Frequency of documenting spontaneous mode in 0–12h is mainly an EMR/monitoring artifact. Not a physiologic renal precursor and low priority.
Documentation frequency in 13–24h is a proxy for care intensity rather than kidney-relevant physiology. Unlikely to add unique value in sparse selection.
Later measurement frequency may weakly track ongoing ventilator management complexity closer to the outcome time. Still a proxy variable and inferior to direct hemodynamic/renal features.

Mean MAP in the first 12h reflects systemic perfusion pressure and risk of renal hypoperfusion after cardiac surgery. Although an average can miss brief hypotensive insults and the window is early/noisy, MAP is a core mechanistic AKI driver and likely to retain value in a sparse model.

Average MAP from arterial line in the 13–24h window reflects overall perfusion but smooths over brief hypotensive insults. Likely redundant with MAP minima (and later windows) that better capture AKI-relevant hypoperfusion for sparse selection.

Average arterial-line MAP in the 25–36h window captures sustained perfusion closer to the Hour-60 outcome. Still somewhat redundant with MAP_min, but may add signal if prolonged low-normal MAP drives renal injury.

Peak MAP in the immediate 0–12h post-op period often reflects transient vasopressor/volume effects rather than renal risk. Provides little unique AKI signal compared with hypotension-focused features and later epochs.
Maximum MAP in 13–24h is more a marker of treatment intensity or hypertension than renal hypoperfusion. Typically dominated by MAP minima/trajectory for predicting creatinine rise.
Peak MAP in the closest window is still not a direct precursor of AKI and is often clinician-driven (pressors) rather than pathophysiology. Unlikely to survive sparse selection versus MAP_min/mean.
Lowest MAP in 0–12h captures early hypotensive episodes that can contribute to renal ischemia. However, immediate post-bypass instability is noisy, so it is less reliable than later MAP minima.
Minimum MAP in 13–24h captures clinically meaningful hypotension after initial post-op turbulence, a strong driver of renal hypoperfusion. Likely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Minimum MAP in 25–36h is the closest extreme-perfusion signal to the Hour-60 creatinine ratio and directly reflects renal hypoperfusion risk. This is the dominant MAP aggregation and a top sparse-model candidate.
Count/frequency of MAP measurements is mainly a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than physiology. In 0–12h it is near-universal with arterial lines, adding minimal unique predictive value.
Measurement frequency reflects clinician attention/acuity rather than perfusion itself. Likely redundant with having an arterial line and with actual MAP values.

MAP measurement frequency in 25–36h is still primarily a care-process proxy (continued ICU intensity) rather than a renal biomarker. Unlikely to outperform direct hypotension metrics in sparse selection.
Proportion of time NPO in 0–12h is routine after cardiac surgery and weakly related to kidney injury. Mostly reflects standard post-op workflow rather than renal physiology.
NPO status in 13–24h is largely driven by extubation/feeding protocols and overall recovery pace. It is an indirect severity proxy and unlikely to add unique value versus hemodynamics/labs.

Persistently NPO by 25–36h may indicate delayed recovery, ongoing ventilation, or instability, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still an indirect care-process marker, so only modest sparse-model priority.
Any NPO in 0–12h is expected post-op and provides little discrimination. Dominated by later-window indicators of delayed recovery if diet status is used at all.
Any NPO in 13–24h remains common and is a weak, indirect proxy for illness severity. Adds little unique predictive information compared with direct perfusion extremes.

Indicates whether the patient was ever NPO during 25–36h post-op. This is mostly a care-process/illness-severity proxy and is redundant with other NPO indicators; closest window gives slight value but unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Whether the patient was continuously NPO in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Early post-op diet status is routine and noisy, offering little unique signal for later creatinine ratio.
Whether the patient was continuously NPO during 13–24h. Still largely reflects feeding progression and clinician choice rather than renal physiology, and is dominated by more proximal 25–36h status.
Whether the patient was continuously NPO during 25–36h. Persistent NPO this late may proxy ongoing instability or procedures, but remains indirect and redundant with other diet/NPO features.
Frequency/availability of NPO documentation in 0–12h. Measurement count is primarily a monitoring/documentation proxy and not a direct renal signal.
Frequency/availability of NPO documentation in 13–24h. Adds little beyond general charting intensity and is unlikely to contribute unique predictive value in a sparse model.
Frequency/availability of NPO documentation in 25–36h. Still mainly reflects documentation/monitoring patterns rather than kidney injury risk.
Average diet status coding in 0–12h (e.g., degree of oral intake). Early post-op feeding is highly protocol-driven and weakly related to AKI risk, making it a low-priority sparse feature.
Average diet status coding in 13–24h. This is an indirect recovery marker and largely redundant with simpler NPO/diet binary indicators.
Average diet status coding in 25–36h. More proximal to the outcome and may weakly reflect recovery/instability, but remains a nonspecific proxy and likely redundant with NPO max/min.
Whether any non-NPO diet occurred in 0–12h. Typically reflects routine post-op progression and provides minimal unique information for predicting creatinine at 60h.
Whether any non-NPO diet occurred in 13–24h. Indirect marker of recovery and dominated by later (25–36h) feeding status if selecting sparsely.
Whether any non-NPO diet occurred in 25–36h. Closest window may capture delayed recovery/ongoing critical illness, but it is still a weak, indirect proxy for renal dysfunction.
Whether the patient was continuously on a non-NPO diet in 0–12h. Rare/early and heavily protocol-dependent, offering little stable signal for later creatinine ratio.
Whether the patient was continuously on a non-NPO diet in 13–24h. Mostly reflects feeding tolerance and care decisions, with limited unique predictive value for AKI.
Whether the patient was continuously on a non-NPO diet in 25–36h. Somewhat informative as a recovery proxy in the most proximal window, but still indirect and redundant with diet_max/NPO indicators.

Count/frequency of documenting diet type in the first 12h, mainly reflecting charting/monitoring intensity. Weak, indirect proxy with high noise early post-op and little unique renal signal for a sparse model.

Diet-type documentation frequency in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior/EMR artifact rather than physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio beyond true hemodynamic/renal features.

Diet-type documentation frequency in 25–36h is still primarily a proxy for care complexity rather than kidney injury. Even though closer to Hour 60, it is not a direct renal or perfusion marker and is low priority.

Average presence/amount of an 'other' diet category in 0–12h likely reflects NPO/feeding orders or documentation quirks. Early post-op diet status is weakly related to AKI and is dominated by more direct renal/perfusion variables.

Mean 'other diet' status in 13–24h is an indirect nutrition/order-set signal. It is unlikely to survive sparse selection because it is nonspecific and confounded by routine post-cardiac surgery pathways.

Mean 'other diet' status in 25–36h may weakly proxy delayed GI recovery/ongoing critical illness, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and likely redundant with stronger severity/hemodynamic features, so low priority.
Whether 'other diet' occurred at any point in 0–12h is mostly a care-pathway/documentation flag. Early timing and nonspecificity make it unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
Any 'other diet' in 13–24h is a binary order/status indicator rather than a physiologic renal precursor. Too indirect and likely redundant with general illness markers.

Any 'other diet' in 25–36h could weakly indicate ongoing critical illness or inability to advance diet, which may track with AKI risk. Still a nonspecific proxy and unlikely to be retained in a sparse linear model if better signals exist.

Minimum of a binary 'other diet' flag in 0–12h is essentially uninformative (often 0 unless always present). Provides no meaningful renal physiology signal and is dominated by the corresponding max/mean if anything.
Minimum of a binary 'other diet' flag in 13–24h is largely a structural artifact and adds little beyond other aggregations. Not a plausible sparse-model keeper for predicting creatinine_ratio.
Minimum of a binary 'other diet' flag in 25–36h is typically 0 unless continuously present, making it low-information. Even with closer timing, it is a weak, redundant proxy.
Frequency of recording the 'other diet' category in 0–12h reflects documentation intensity rather than patient physiology. Early post-op and proxy-heavy, so very low sparse-selection priority.

Measurement frequency of 'other diet' in 13–24h is an EMR process variable. It is unlikely to contribute unique predictive information about evolving AKI compared with direct renal/hemodynamic features.
Measurement frequency of 'other diet' in 25–36h remains a monitoring/documentation proxy. Despite temporal proximity, it is not a direct kidney injury signal and is low value for sparse regression.

Mean indicator of regular cardiac rhythm in 0–12h may weakly relate to perfusion stability (arrhythmias can reduce renal blood flow). However it is early, indirect, and likely redundant with direct hemodynamics/vasopressor needs, so low-moderate priority.

Represents proportion of time in a regular rhythm during 13–24h (inverse of arrhythmia burden). Only an indirect hemodynamic/illness marker for AKI risk and largely redundant with the irregular indicators and later (25–36h) window.

Represents proportion of time in a regular rhythm during 25–36h, closer to the creatinine outcome. Still an indirect proxy (arrhythmia/instability) and mostly redundant with irregular rhythm features; unlikely to survive sparse selection unless rhythm is a key driver in this dataset.

Binary indicator that the patient ever had a regular rhythm in 0–12h. Early post-op rhythm is noisy and this ‘max’ is uninformative (most patients will have some regular rhythm), so it adds little unique predictive value.

Binary indicator of any regular rhythm occurrence in 13–24h. This is a weak, ceiling-prone summary and is dominated by more informative measures of irregular rhythm burden and later-window physiology.
Binary indicator of any regular rhythm occurrence in 25–36h. Despite better timing, it remains a low-information, near-constant feature and is redundant with irregular rhythm summaries.

Binary indicator that regular rhythm was present at the minimum (i.e., throughout) in 0–12h. Early post-op period is highly confounded by transient factors, and this is still an indirect proxy for renal risk.

Binary indicator suggesting sustained regular rhythm across 13–24h (low arrhythmia burden). Somewhat more meaningful than ‘max’, but still indirect for AKI and redundant with irregular rhythm measures.

Binary indicator suggesting sustained regular rhythm across 25–36h, the closest window. Could reflect ongoing stability, but remains an indirect signal and likely dominated by direct perfusion/renal markers in a sparse model.

Count/frequency of regularity measurements in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity. This is a clinician-behavior proxy and early-window noise makes it unlikely to add stable predictive value.
Measurement frequency of rhythm regularity in 13–24h, primarily a proxy for acuity/attention. Generally redundant with other severity indicators and not a direct physiologic renal signal.

Measurement frequency of rhythm regularity in 25–36h, reflecting monitoring/concern rather than physiology. Even with better timing, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection versus direct hemodynamic/renal variables.

Represents average irregular rhythm burden in 0–12h (e.g., AF/ectopy), which can relate to low output and renal hypoperfusion. However, early post-op arrhythmias are common/transient and less reliable for Hour-60 creatinine prediction.

Represents irregular rhythm burden in 13–24h, a more informative trajectory window. This can capture sustained arrhythmia-related hemodynamic compromise and may add unique value beyond ‘regular’ summaries.

Represents irregular rhythm burden in 25–36h, closest to the outcome and more likely to reflect ongoing low-output/instability affecting renal perfusion. Among this batch, it is the most informative rhythm-derived continuous summary and thus the best sparse-model candidate.
Binary indicator of any irregular rhythm in 0–12h. This is common early after cardiac surgery and the ‘any occurrence’ aggregation is low-specificity and redundant with mean burden measures.

Binary indicator of any irregular rhythm in 13–24h. More meaningful than 0–12h but still a coarse, ceiling-prone proxy that is generally dominated by the continuous irregular_mean (especially 25–36h).

Indicates any occurrence of irregular rhythm (e.g., AF) in the 25–36h window, which can affect renal perfusion via hemodynamic instability. Closest time window and a useful marker, but it is an indirect AKI predictor and largely a severity/hemodynamics proxy rather than a direct renal signal.

Early (0–12h) indicator of irregular rhythm presence/absence, a period with substantial post-bypass/anesthesia noise. Likely redundant with later-window irregularity and weak for Hour-60 creatinine prediction in a sparse model.

Presence/absence of irregular rhythm in 13–24h may reflect evolving AF and associated hemodynamic stress. Some predictive value, but still indirect and dominated by the nearer 25–36h irregularity summary.

Presence/absence of irregular rhythm in the 25–36h window captures ongoing arrhythmia burden close to the target time. Useful as a sparse candidate but still an indirect proxy and partially redundant with the corresponding max indicator.

Count/frequency of documenting irregular rhythm in 0–12h is primarily monitoring intensity/clinical attention rather than physiology. Early window and measurement-frequency nature make it unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency of irregular rhythm in 13–24h is mainly a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy. Adds little unique renal predictive signal beyond whether irregularity occurred.
Measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly reflect instability prompting closer monitoring. Still largely a proxy and typically dominated by the binary occurrence features for sparse modeling.
Represents average proportion/time in a non-sinus, non-irregular 'other' rhythm category early post-op. Early timing and ambiguous clinical meaning make it noisy and low-yield for sparse selection.

Indicates average presence of 'other' rhythm in 13–24h, a heterogeneous category (e.g., paced rhythms) with unclear linkage to AKI. Likely redundant with more interpretable rhythm/hemodynamic variables and low unique value.

Average burden of 'other' rhythm in 25–36h could reflect pacing/arrhythmias associated with low output states. Closer window helps, but the category is nonspecific and likely weaker than irregular-rhythm indicators.
Any occurrence of 'other' rhythm in 0–12h is common post-op (e.g., pacing) and often transient. Early window and nonspecific rhythm category make it a poor sparse-model candidate.

Any 'other' rhythm occurrence in 13–24h may signal ongoing conduction issues/pacing needs. Some indirect severity information, but nonspecific and typically dominated by clearer hemodynamic/renal predictors.
Any 'other' rhythm in 25–36h may indicate persistent pacing or atypical rhythms that can correlate with low cardiac output. Still nonspecific and indirect for AKI, so moderate-low priority.

Presence/absence of 'other' rhythm in 0–12h is heavily influenced by immediate post-op management (temporary pacing) and transient effects. Low specificity and early timing reduce sparse-selection value.

Presence/absence of 'other' rhythm in 13–24h may reflect persistent pacing/conduction abnormalities. Indirect and nonspecific, but could add small incremental signal if rhythm variables are otherwise limited.

Presence/absence of 'other' rhythm in 25–36h captures persistent non-sinus rhythms closest to the target. However, the category is heterogeneous and likely redundant with stronger hemodynamic/arrhythmia indicators.
Count/frequency of documenting an 'other' cardiac rhythm regularity in the first 12h. This is mainly a monitoring/documentation proxy and nonspecific for renal injury, so low sparse-model priority.

Measurement frequency of an 'other' rhythm regularity category during 13–24h. Largely reflects clinician attention/EMR coding rather than a direct hemodynamic or renal signal, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of an 'other' rhythm regularity category during 25–36h. Slightly more temporally relevant, but still mostly a documentation/acuity proxy and weakly linked to creatinine trajectory.

Average heart rate from pulse-ox monitor in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. HR is an indirect illness/hemodynamic stress marker and early values are noisy post-bypass, so limited unique value for AKI prediction.

Mean pulse-ox-derived heart rate during 13–24h. Some signal for sustained stress/low output states, but still indirect and typically dominated by more renal-proximal features (e.g., urine output/creatinine) in sparse models.

Mean pulse-ox-derived heart rate during 25–36h, closest window to the 60h creatinine outcome. Persistent tachycardia can reflect ongoing shock/low cardiac output and may add modest unique predictive value despite being indirect.
Peak pulse-ox-derived heart rate in 0–12h. Extremes can capture acute stress/arrhythmia, but early post-op peaks are often transient and nonspecific for later creatinine rise.
Maximum pulse-ox-derived heart rate during 13–24h. Captures episodic tachycardia but remains an indirect proxy and is often redundant with mean HR or other hemodynamic/vasopressor variables.

Maximum pulse-ox-derived heart rate during 25–36h. A late extreme may better reflect ongoing instability (e.g., low output, sepsis, AF with RVR) and can add incremental signal beyond the mean in a sparse model.
Minimum pulse-ox-derived heart rate in 0–12h. Bradycardic minima are often artifact/sedation/pacing-related early post-op and are weakly connected to evolving AKI, so low priority.

Minimum pulse-ox-derived heart rate during 13–24h. Typically reflects pacing/sedation or transient events and provides little unique renal predictive information compared with other hemodynamic markers.

Minimum pulse-ox-derived heart rate during 25–36h. Later bradycardia could indicate conduction issues/low output, but it is still nonspecific and usually less informative than tachycardic extremes for AKI risk.
Number of pulse-ox heart rate measurements in 0–12h. This is primarily monitoring intensity/workflow and not a physiologic renal signal, so very low sparse-model value.
Measurement frequency of pulse-ox heart rate during 13–24h. Mostly reflects nursing/monitoring patterns and patient complexity rather than kidney injury mechanisms.

Measurement frequency of pulse-ox heart rate during 25–36h. Slightly more informative as an acuity proxy closer to outcome, but still indirect and generally dominated by actual physiologic extremes/means.
Average apical (auscultated) heart rate in 0–12h. Similar information to monitor-derived HR with added redundancy and early post-op noise, so unlikely to be selected when feature budget is tight.

Average apical heart rate in 13–24h reflects general physiologic stress but is an indirect AKI signal. Mean HR is usually redundant with stronger perfusion/hemodynamic variables and less informative than extremes.

Average apical heart rate in 25–36h is closer to the creatinine outcome window but still a nonspecific severity marker. In a sparse model it is typically dominated by direct renal/perfusion features and by HR extremes if present.

Indicator of high apical HR early (0–12h) captures immediate post-op tachycardia, often driven by transient anesthesia/bypass effects. Early-window extremes are noisy and provide limited unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Indicator of tachycardia (apical) in 13–24h may reflect evolving shock/pain/arrhythmia but remains indirect for renal injury. As a binary extreme it is a coarse proxy and likely redundant with BP/vasopressor/lactate signals.

Indicator of tachycardia (apical) in 25–36h is temporally closer and may mark ongoing low-output state or sepsis that can precede AKI. Still indirect and coarse, but the late-window extreme could survive screening if perfusion variables are limited.

Indicator of low apical HR in 0–12h often reflects pacing, beta-blockade, sedation, or immediate post-op management rather than renal hypoperfusion. Early and highly confounded, so low sparse-selection priority.
Indicator of low apical HR in 13–24h is frequently driven by pacing/medications and does not specifically encode renal risk. Likely redundant and confounded, making it a weak sparse-model candidate.

Indicator of low apical HR in 25–36h could reflect persistent conduction issues or heavy beta-blockade with potential low cardiac output. Still nonspecific and confounded, but later timing gives slightly more potential signal than earlier epochs.
Count/frequency of apical HR measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinical workflow proxy. It is not a physiologic renal marker and is unlikely to add stable predictive value.
Apical HR measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician attention/acuity rather than kidney physiology. Typically noisy and dominated by actual hemodynamic values in sparse selection.
Apical HR measurement frequency in 25–36h is a proxy for monitoring intensity and care complexity. Even though later, it remains indirect and usually not worth a sparse feature slot.

Average peripheral HR in 0–12h is an early, nonspecific stress marker and may be affected by artifact/measurement source differences. Redundant with apical HR summaries and unlikely to help predict Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Peripheral HR mean (13–24h) provides little unique information beyond apical HR and other hemodynamic variables. As a binary-typed mean it is also a coarse representation, lowering sparse-model utility.

Peripheral HR mean (25–36h) is closer to the outcome window but remains an indirect severity marker and redundant with apical HR. Could add marginal signal if apical data are missing, otherwise low priority.

Indicator of peripheral tachycardia in 0–12h is prone to immediate post-op noise and source/measurement artifact. Provides minimal unique renal-risk information compared with later or more direct perfusion markers.

Indicator of peripheral tachycardia in 13–24h may reflect ongoing stress/low output but is still indirect for AKI. Likely redundant with apical HR max and other shock markers, so only a weak-to-moderate sparse candidate.

Indicates whether the heart rate was sourced from a peripheral signal in 25–36h (max over window). This is a monitoring/measurement-source artifact rather than physiology and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Binary indicator of peripheral source for heart rate in 0–12h (min over window). It is a clinician/device behavior proxy and early post-op timing further reduces usefulness for Hour-60 renal prediction.
Binary indicator of peripheral heart-rate source in 13–24h. Still a documentation/monitoring-source feature with minimal direct linkage to AKI risk and low sparse-model priority.
Binary indicator of peripheral heart-rate source in 25–36h. Despite closer timing, it remains a weak proxy for monitoring setup rather than renal perfusion or injury.
Count/frequency of peripheral-source heart rate measurements in 0–12h. Measurement frequency is mainly monitoring intensity and early post-op noise; unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Frequency of peripheral-source heart rate measurements in 13–24h. This is a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy and provides little unique information about evolving kidney dysfunction.
Frequency of peripheral-source heart rate measurements in 25–36h. Even in the closest window, it reflects monitoring configuration rather than a renal-relevant physiologic signal.

Proportion/average time on mechanical ventilation in 0–12h. It is an indirect severity marker and early post-op ventilation is common after cardiac surgery, limiting unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Average exposure to mechanical ventilation in 13–24h. Persistent ventilation beyond the immediate post-op period can reflect ongoing shock/respiratory failure and may add some independent AKI-risk signal.

Average exposure to mechanical ventilation in 25–36h. Continued need for mechanical ventilation this late is a strong marker of ongoing critical illness/hemodynamic stress and is more likely to contribute unique predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Whether mechanical ventilation occurred at any point in 0–12h. Nearly universal immediately post-cardiac surgery, so it is low-information and dominated by later-window ventilation features.
Whether mechanical ventilation occurred at any point in 13–24h. More informative than 0–12h but still a coarse binary dominated by the 25–36h mean (duration/persistence) for sparse selection.

Whether mechanical ventilation occurred at any point in 25–36h. Closer to the target and indicates failure to extubate, but as a binary it is less informative than the corresponding mean exposure in the same window.

Whether mechanical ventilation was present throughout the entire 0–12h window (min over window). Early continuous ventilation is expected post-op and provides little discrimination for later creatinine rise.
Whether mechanical ventilation was continuous across 13–24h. Suggests prolonged ventilation but is still a coarse proxy and partially redundant with later-window ventilation persistence.

Whether mechanical ventilation was continuous across 25–36h. Indicates sustained critical illness close to the prediction horizon, but is somewhat redundant with 25–36h mechanical_mean which better captures exposure intensity.

Frequency/amount of documentation of mechanical ventilation in the immediate post-op window, largely reflecting routine care and monitoring intensity. Indirect severity/proxy signal and early (0–12h) noise make it a weak sparse-model candidate.

Measurement frequency for being on mechanical ventilation during 13–24h, a proxy for prolonged respiratory support and clinician attention. Still indirect for AKI and likely redundant with more direct hemodynamic/renal features, so low priority.

Measurement frequency for mechanical ventilation closest to the target window, capturing persistence of critical illness/ongoing ventilation. More temporally relevant than earlier epochs but remains a proxy variable, so only moderate sparse value.

Average proportion of time with no oxygen device in the first 12h, which is uncommon and heavily influenced by immediate post-op workflow. Weak and noisy early signal and largely redundant with later oxygen/ventilation status.

Average time off oxygen support in 13–24h, reflecting earlier recovery/less respiratory support. Indirect for renal outcomes and likely dominated by closer (25–36h) respiratory status, so low priority.

Average time with no oxygen device in 25–36h, indicating recovery and lower illness severity closer to Hour 60. Still an indirect severity marker, but timing makes it a more plausible sparse predictor than earlier means.

Whether the patient ever had no oxygen device in 0–12h (a transient/extreme indicator). Early post-op extubation/room-air episodes are workflow-dependent and noisy, offering little unique renal predictive value.
Any occurrence of no oxygen device in 13–24h, suggesting some respiratory stability. Indirect for AKI and likely redundant with the closer 25–36h status, so weak-to-moderate priority.

Any occurrence of no oxygen device in 25–36h, a closer-window marker of recovery and lower ongoing critical illness. More informative than mean/min in sparse selection, but still a non-renal proxy signal.

Whether the patient was ever not on 'none' (i.e., required some oxygen device) in 0–12h; in practice most post-cardiac surgery patients will, making it low-variance. Early epoch and near-ubiquity limit unique predictive value.
Whether the patient consistently had no oxygen device throughout 13–24h (a stringent recovery marker). Potentially informative but rare and still indirect for renal dysfunction, so low priority.

Whether the patient maintained no oxygen device throughout 25–36h, indicating sustained respiratory stability close to the prediction horizon. More temporally relevant and less noisy than earlier epochs, but remains an indirect severity proxy.
Measurement frequency for 'no oxygen device' in 0–12h, primarily reflecting documentation patterns rather than physiology. Proxy-heavy and early-window noise make it a poor sparse-model feature.
Measurement frequency for 'no oxygen device' in 13–24h, mostly a monitoring/documentation artifact. Adds little unique information beyond actual oxygen-device status variables.
Measurement frequency for 'no oxygen device' in 25–36h, which may weakly reflect stability and lower monitoring intensity. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy, so only slight sparse-model value.

Average time on low-flow oxygen in the first 12h, reflecting routine post-op respiratory support and mild illness severity. Early timing and indirect relationship to AKI make it a weak-to-moderate candidate.

Represents typical use of low-flow oxygen in the 13–24h window (a mild respiratory support/trajectory marker). It is an indirect illness-severity proxy and likely redundant with the closer 25–36h oxygen-support indicators, so low sparse-model priority.

Represents typical low-flow oxygen use in the 25–36h window. Closer to the outcome but still a weak, indirect proxy for overall recovery rather than a direct renal signal; may add little beyond stronger hemodynamic/renal features.

Indicates any low-flow oxygen use in the immediate 0–12h post-op period. Early post-op oxygen device choice is noisy and heavily protocol-driven, offering minimal unique information for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Indicates any low-flow oxygen use during 13–24h. Still largely reflects routine step-down respiratory support and is a nonspecific severity proxy, with limited unique predictive value for AKI compared with more direct renal/perfusion variables.

Indicates any low-flow oxygen use during 25–36h. Timing is favorable, but the signal is nonspecific and likely redundant with other respiratory-support markers; modest value at best for sparse selection.

Binary minimum for low-flow oxygen in 0–12h is essentially a redundant encoding of device presence/absence. It is dominated by later-window support patterns and provides little renal-specific information.
Binary minimum for low-flow oxygen in 13–24h is a redundant representation of low-flow use. Adds negligible unique signal beyond max/mean and remains an indirect proxy for illness severity.

Binary minimum for low-flow oxygen in 25–36h is redundant and poorly informative (device presence coding). Even with better timing, it is unlikely to survive sparse selection versus more direct kidney/perfusion predictors.
Counts/reflects measurement frequency/documentation of low-flow oxygen in 0–12h. This is primarily a monitoring/documentation intensity proxy and early post-op noise, so very low priority.

Measurement frequency of low-flow oxygen in 13–24h mainly captures charting intensity and care complexity. It is not a physiologic renal marker and is unlikely to add stable predictive value in a sparse model.

Measurement frequency of low-flow oxygen in 25–36h is a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy. Despite proximity to Hour 60, it is not a direct kidney signal and is typically dominated by actual physiologic variables.

Represents high-flow oxygen use in 0–12h, suggesting early respiratory failure and higher acuity. However, it is early and can be transient/protocol-driven post-op, making it a weaker and indirect predictor of later creatinine rise.

Represents high-flow oxygen requirement in 13–24h, a stronger marker of persistent cardiopulmonary dysfunction and systemic illness. Still indirect for AKI, but more likely than low-flow variables to add unique severity/trajectory information.

Represents high-flow oxygen requirement in 25–36h, close to the outcome and indicative of ongoing significant illness that can correlate with renal hypoperfusion/inflammation. Indirect but potentially useful as a sparse-model severity/trajectory feature.

Indicates any high-flow oxygen use in 0–12h, capturing an early extreme of respiratory support. The extreme is more informative than a mean, but early post-op instability limits reliability for Hour-60 creatinine prediction.

Indicates any high-flow oxygen use in 13–24h, capturing an extreme respiratory-support need that reflects higher acuity. While still indirect for renal injury, this extreme is more likely to survive sparse selection than low-flow or measured proxies.

Represents any use of high-flow oxygen in the 25–36h window (respiratory support severity). It is an indirect illness-severity proxy for AKI risk and likely redundant with more direct hemodynamic/renal features, so low sparse-model priority.

Indicates whether high-flow oxygen was present at any point early (0–12h), a period with substantial immediate post-op noise. Weak, indirect signal for later creatinine rise and dominated by later-window respiratory support if needed.
Captures any high-flow oxygen use in 13–24h as a marker of respiratory support needs. Still indirect for renal dysfunction and largely redundant with the closer 25–36h window indicator.

Any high-flow oxygen use in the closest window (25–36h) may reflect ongoing cardiopulmonary dysfunction that can co-occur with renal hypoperfusion. Indirect and likely redundant with other severity markers, but timing makes it slightly more useful than earlier epochs.

Frequency/availability of documentation for high-flow oxygen in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/EMR process signal. Poor physiologic specificity and early timing make it unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement count for high-flow oxygen in 13–24h reflects documentation intensity rather than a direct biomarker. Adds little unique predictive value beyond the binary presence/absence features.
Measurement frequency in 25–36h is primarily a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy. Even though closer to Hour 60, it is less informative than whether high-flow oxygen was actually used.

Documentation frequency of first-degree AV block in 0–12h is a process/monitoring proxy and often clinically minor. Early post-op conduction changes are common and weakly linked to AKI, so very low priority.
Measurement count for first-degree AV block in 13–24h reflects charting intensity more than physiology. Provides minimal unique information for predicting creatinine ratio.
Measurement frequency of first-degree AV block in 25–36h is not a direct renal signal and is largely a documentation proxy. Unlikely to add value in a sparse linear model.

Measurement frequency for higher-grade AV block (2nd/3rd degree) may correlate with hemodynamic instability and need for pacing/pressors. Still a proxy (measured) and early-window noise limits its sparse-model value.

Documentation frequency of 2nd/3rd degree AV block in 13–24h can reflect clinically significant conduction issues associated with low output states. Indirect and process-heavy, so only weak-to-moderate priority.

Measurement frequency of 2nd/3rd degree AV block in 25–36h may indicate persistent conduction-related instability closer to the outcome window. Still not a direct renal biomarker, but timing and potential link to low cardiac output make it the strongest among the heart-block measured features.

Measurement frequency of bundle branch block in 0–12h is largely a documentation artifact and often reflects baseline conduction disease. Weak and nonspecific for AKI prediction, especially early post-op.
Documentation frequency of bundle branch block in 13–24h is not a direct physiologic driver of renal injury. Likely redundant with broader cardiac dysfunction markers and mostly process-related.
Measurement frequency of bundle branch block in 25–36h remains a monitoring proxy and often chronic rather than an acute insult. Adds little unique predictive value for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Average ionized calcium in the immediate post-op window, reflecting transfusion/citrate load and replacement practices. Likely indirect for AKI and dominated by later-window or extreme calcium abnormalities, so low sparse-model priority.

Mean ionized calcium during early ICU course, capturing ongoing electrolyte management and physiologic stability. Still an indirect renal signal and likely redundant with min/max in the closer 25–36h window.

Mean ionized calcium closest to the prediction horizon, reflecting sustained calcium derangements and ongoing critical illness. More temporally relevant than earlier means, but still a secondary/indirect predictor versus extremes.
Peak ionized calcium immediately post-op, often influenced by supplementation and lab timing. Weak mechanistic link to later creatinine rise and likely noisy in the 0–12h window.

Maximum ionized calcium in 13–24h, typically reflecting treatment rather than renal pathophysiology. Adds little unique value for AKI prediction compared with hypocalcemia (min) or later-window signals.

Peak ionized calcium in the closest window, potentially reflecting aggressive replacement or metabolic shifts. Some temporal relevance, but hypercalcemia is generally a weaker/less specific AKI precursor than low calcium or hemodynamic/renal markers.

Lowest ionized calcium early post-op, capturing citrate effect, dilution, and physiologic stress that can track with severity. Extremes are more informative than means, but 0–12h abnormalities are often transient/noisy for Hour-60 creatinine.

Minimum ionized calcium in 13–24h, reflecting persistent hypocalcemia and ongoing critical illness/organ dysfunction. Moderately useful as an extreme-value severity signal, though still indirect for kidney injury.

Minimum ionized calcium closest to Hour 60, capturing persistent hypocalcemia that can correlate with evolving organ dysfunction and higher-risk trajectories. Among calcium features, this extreme in the latest window is most likely to survive sparse selection.

Count/frequency of ionized calcium measurements in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and protocolized post-op care. Primarily a clinician-behavior proxy with limited unique predictive value.

Measurement frequency of ionized calcium in 13–24h, reflecting clinician concern/complexity rather than physiology. Generally redundant with other acuity indicators and low priority for sparse models.

Ionized calcium measurement frequency in 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability or electrolyte management late in the observed window. Still a proxy variable, but slightly more informative due to temporal proximity to the outcome.

Average magnesium immediately post-op, heavily influenced by routine repletion and bypass-related shifts. Weak and nonspecific for later creatinine ratio, and early means are particularly prone to noise.

Mean magnesium in 13–24h, reflecting ongoing replacement and renal handling. Some indirect relevance (renal excretion), but likely modest and dominated by later-window or extreme magnesium values (if available).

Mean magnesium in 25–36h, closer to the target and potentially reflecting reduced renal clearance or sustained supplementation needs. More likely to add signal than earlier means, though still indirect compared with direct renal/hemodynamic markers.
Peak magnesium in the immediate post-op period, often driven by bolus repletion and peri-bypass practices. Early maxima are noisy and not a strong, specific precursor of AKI for sparse selection.

Peak magnesium in 13–24h reflects supplementation/renal handling but is usually clinician-driven and weakly tied to evolving AKI. Likely redundant with later (25–36h) magnesium extremes and stronger renal/hemodynamic signals.

Peak magnesium in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect reduced renal clearance or aggressive repletion in sicker patients. Still an indirect marker and often treatment-confounded, but the latest extreme is the most defensible magnesium summary for sparse selection.
Early nadir magnesium is heavily influenced by bypass-related shifts and routine repletion immediately post-op. High noise and timing is distant from Hour 60, so low priority in a sparse model.

Magnesium nadir in 13–24h may reflect ongoing losses or under-repletion, but remains largely management-driven and nonspecific for AKI. Some signal, but dominated by later window and by more direct renal perfusion/acid-base markers.

Latest magnesium nadir can capture persistent electrolyte derangements and may correlate with illness severity/renal dysfunction closer to Hour 60. Indirect and confounded, but among magnesium features this is the most potentially informative for sparse inclusion.

Count/frequency of magnesium measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity proxy in the immediate post-op period. Weak direct physiologic information and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement frequency reflects clinician concern and care patterns more than kidney physiology. Adds little unique predictive value beyond actual magnesium values and other severity markers.
More frequent magnesium checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability or renal/electrolyte issues closer to the target time. Still primarily a behavior proxy, so only modest priority.

Average base excess in 0–12h summarizes early metabolic acidosis/alkalosis but is confounded by bypass, ventilation, and transfusion effects. Mean also smooths short insults, making it a weaker sparse-model candidate than later extremes.

Mean base excess in 13–24h reflects sustained metabolic derangement and can track evolving shock/poor perfusion relevant to AKI risk. Useful but somewhat redundant with base-excess minima/maxima and later window summaries.

Mean base excess in 25–36h captures persistent metabolic acidosis/alkalosis close to Hour 60, a strong marker of ongoing hypoperfusion/critical illness linked to AKI. Among means, this timing is most likely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.

Maximum base excess early post-op often reflects transient alkalosis from ventilation/bicarbonate and is less tied to renal hypoperfusion. Early timing and directionality make it low-yield versus minima and later windows.

Peak base excess in 13–24h may indicate alkalosis from diuresis/ventilation or bicarbonate therapy, which is less directly predictive of AKI than acidosis. Likely dominated by base_excess_min and later (25–36h) summaries.

Peak base excess in 25–36h captures late alkalosis but is generally less pathophysiologically linked to AKI than negative base excess. Adds limited unique value compared with base_excess_mean/min in the same window.

Minimum base excess in 0–12h captures worst early metabolic acidosis, a potential marker of hypoperfusion that can precede AKI. However, early post-bypass noise reduces reliability for Hour 60 compared with later minima.

Minimum base excess in 13–24h captures significant metabolic acidosis after initial post-op stabilization, a strong signal of ongoing hypoperfusion/inflammation associated with AKI. As an extreme, it is more informative than the mean and less redundant than base-excess maxima.

Minimum base excess in the 25–36h window captures the worst metabolic acidosis, often reflecting hypoperfusion/shock that can precede AKI. As a late-window extreme it is a strong, relatively nonredundant severity-of-illness signal for Hour-60 creatinine rise.

Count/frequency of base excess measurements in 0–12h reflects monitoring intensity rather than physiology. Early post-op measurement frequency is a weak, proxy-heavy predictor and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Measurement frequency of base excess in 13–24h is primarily clinician behavior/acuity proxy, not a direct renal or perfusion biomarker. Adds little unique value versus actual base excess values/extremes.

Late-window base excess measurement frequency may weakly indicate ongoing instability prompting repeated ABGs. Still largely a monitoring proxy and dominated by the actual base excess extreme (e.g., base_excess_min).
Frequency of direct bilirubin checks in 0–12h is a monitoring/order-pattern proxy and not a direct kidney injury marker. Early timing and indirectness make it low priority for a sparse model.

Direct bilirubin measurement frequency in 13–24h mainly reflects clinician concern for hepatic dysfunction/hemolysis rather than renal physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.

Late measurement frequency of direct bilirubin can proxy evolving multiorgan dysfunction, which correlates with AKI risk. Still indirect and behavior-driven, so lower priority than physiologic extremes.
Average WBC in 0–12h reflects early inflammatory/stress response after surgery and is often nonspecific. Mean aggregation and early timing make it weak and likely redundant with later WBC summaries.
Mean WBC in 13–24h may reflect evolving infection/inflammation that can contribute to AKI risk. However it is nonspecific and typically dominated by later window and/or extreme WBC measures.

Mean WBC in 25–36h captures sustained inflammation closer to the outcome window, which can correlate with AKI. Still a generic severity marker and less informative than extremes, so moderate-low priority.

Peak WBC in 0–12h captures early stress leukocytosis but is heavily influenced by immediate post-op physiology. As an early extreme it has some signal but is less reliable for Hour-60 creatinine than later peaks.

Peak WBC in 13–24h may better reflect clinically meaningful inflammation/infection trajectory associated with AKI risk. As an extreme it is more informative than mean/min and can add some unique value.

Peak WBC in 25–36h is the closest extreme inflammatory marker to the target time and may capture evolving sepsis/SIRS contributing to renal dysfunction. Still indirect for kidney injury, so not top-tier but a reasonable sparse-model candidate.
Minimum WBC in 0–12h is rarely a direct driver of AKI and may reflect dilutional effects or lab variability early post-op. Likely redundant and low yield for sparse selection.
Minimum WBC in 13–24h is a nonspecific marker and generally less informative than WBC max for acute inflammatory burden. Low unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
Minimum WBC in 25–36h is not a strong mechanistic precursor of AKI and is typically dominated by WBC max/other severity signals. Unlikely to be retained in a sparse linear model.
Count of WBC lab draws in the first 12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity rather than physiology. Weak, proxy-heavy signal and early window; unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Frequency of WBC checks in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy. Adds little unique renal information and is dominated by direct hemodynamic/renal markers.

Later (25–36h) WBC measurement frequency may reflect evolving complications prompting closer monitoring. Still an indirect proxy and generally redundant with actual lab values/organ support variables, so low priority.

Average PT in the immediate post-op period reflects coagulopathy/transfusion/hepatic dysfunction and bypass effects. Early and mean-aggregated, so it is a noisy, indirect predictor of AKI compared with later/extreme values.

Mean PT at 25–36h captures persistent coagulopathy/illness severity closer to the creatinine outcome. Moderately useful but indirect for renal injury and likely redundant with stronger shock/organ dysfunction markers.

Peak PT in 0–12h may indicate severe early coagulopathy (bleeding, massive transfusion, hepatic hypoperfusion). Extremes are better than means, but the early window is confounded by intraop factors, limiting sparse-model value.

Peak PT at 25–36h signals ongoing/worsening coagulopathy and systemic dysfunction near-term to Hour 60, often correlating with hypoperfusion and AKI risk. Among PT variants, this extreme in the latest window is the most informative and least redundant.

Lowest PT early post-op is usually not a meaningful renal-risk signal and can reflect lab variability or correction with products. Dominated by max/PT trajectory measures for capturing pathologic insult.
Minimum PT in 25–36h is generally less clinically informative than maximum/prolongation for organ dysfunction. Low unique predictive value for creatinine ratio in a sparse model.
Number of PT measurements in 0–12h reflects monitoring/transfusion workflows rather than kidney physiology. Strongly proxy-like and early, so very low selection priority.
PT measurement frequency in 13–24h is primarily clinician behavior/acuity. Adds little beyond actual PT values and other severity indicators.
More frequent PT checks at 25–36h may indicate ongoing bleeding/coagulopathy management and higher illness complexity. Still indirect and typically redundant with PT max itself, so only modest value.
Mean NRBCs early can reflect hypoxia/stress response and severe systemic illness. However it is nonspecific, early, and often redundant with lactate/vasopressors/hemodynamics for AKI prediction.
NRBCs persisting into 13–24h suggest ongoing hypoxic stress and higher severity, which can track with AKI risk. Moderately useful but still indirect and likely correlated with other severity markers.

Sustained NRBC elevation at 25–36h indicates ongoing hypoxia/inflammation and severe physiologic stress close to the outcome window. Provides a relatively unique severity signal that may add value in sparse selection when direct renal markers are limited.
Peak NRBCs in 0–12h captures an extreme stress/hypoxia signal, but early postoperative values can be transient and confounded. Less reliable for Hour 60 creatinine than later persistence measures.

Peak NRBCs reflect physiologic stress/hypoxemia/inflammation rather than a direct renal marker. Some association with severity and AKI risk, but likely weak and dominated by more kidney-specific/hemodynamic features in a sparse model.

Later peak NRBCs may better capture ongoing critical illness closer to the 60h creatinine outcome. Still an indirect severity signal and unlikely to add unique predictive value versus direct renal perfusion/renal function variables.
Minimum NRBCs in the immediate post-op window is largely uninformative and may reflect lab noise/absence of NRBCs. Early timing and the 'min' aggregation make it a poor sparse-model candidate.
Minimum NRBCs is not a meaningful pathophysiologic extreme for AKI prediction (often just zero). Redundant and low-yield compared with max or other organ-perfusion markers.
Minimum NRBCs late post-op is typically a floor effect and does not capture evolving insult. Very unlikely to survive sparse selection given weak, indirect linkage to creatinine rise.

NRBC measurement frequency is mainly a monitoring/ordering-intensity proxy rather than physiology. Early post-op ordering patterns are especially noisy and not uniquely predictive of 60h creatinine ratio.
Counts how often NRBCs were checked, reflecting clinician concern/complexity more than renal injury. Low priority for sparse selection due to proxy nature and likely confounding.
