Later measurement frequency may indicate ongoing instability or hematologic evaluation closer to the outcome window. Still primarily a behavior proxy, so only modest value if sparse features are limited.

Average glucose early post-op reflects stress response/insulin therapy and general illness severity. Mean in 0–12h is prone to transient peri-bypass effects and is usually dominated by later or extreme glucose metrics.

Sustained hyperglycemia can correlate with complications and AKI risk, but mean glucose is an indirect marker and influenced by treatment. Likely redundant with max glucose and with later (25–36h) glycemic control.

Mean glucose in 25–36h captures sustained dysglycemia closer to the 60h creatinine outcome and may reflect ongoing stress/vasopressor use/infection risk. Still indirect for kidney injury, but the timing makes it the best mean-glucose candidate in this batch.

Peak glucose early post-op captures acute stress hyperglycemia but is heavily influenced by perioperative steroids, bypass, and immediate management. Early timing reduces reliability for 60h creatinine prediction and it is replaceable by later peaks.
Peak glucose in 13–24h better reflects early trajectory and stress burden beyond immediate post-op noise. As an extreme it is more informative than mean, but remains an indirect AKI predictor.

Peak glucose in 25–36h is the closest extreme glycemic stress signal to the 60h outcome and may capture ongoing critical illness and inflammatory/catecholamine states linked to AKI. Within the glucose family, this is the most likely to survive sparse selection (extreme + best timing).

Minimum glucose early post-op mainly reflects treatment-related hypoglycemia risk and measurement artifacts rather than renal pathophysiology. Low unique value and early timing make it a poor sparse-model feature.

Minimum glucose in 13–24h may capture clinically significant hypoglycemia (marker of frailty/over-treatment) that can correlate with worse outcomes. Still indirect for AKI and generally less informative than max glucose for stress-related renal risk.

Minimum glucose in the closest (25–36h) window captures hypoglycemia/stress dysglycemia, which can track severity and catecholamine/insulin exposure. Moderately useful but indirect for AKI and likely dominated by hemodynamics/renal-specific markers not in this batch.

Count/frequency of glucose measurements in the immediate post-op window is mainly a monitoring/insulin-protocol intensity proxy. Weak direct renal signal and early timing is noisy, so low sparse-model priority.
Glucose measurement frequency reflects clinician behavior and illness complexity more than physiology. Slightly later window but still a weak, proxy-heavy predictor for creatinine ratio.

Later glucose measurement frequency may indicate ongoing instability or tight glycemic control needs closer to the outcome horizon. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy, so only modest value for sparse selection.

Average platelet count early after surgery reflects CPB-related consumption/hemodilution and general inflammatory response. Indirect for AKI and early window is noisy; likely redundant with later platelet summaries.
Mean platelets at 13–24h may reflect evolving coagulopathy/inflammation and overall severity. Still indirect for renal dysfunction and dominated by more informative extremes and later timing.

Mean platelet count in the 25–36h window captures sustained thrombocytopenia/inflammation closer to Hour 60. Moderately useful but less sensitive than minima for acute consumptive processes and remains an indirect AKI marker.
Maximum platelet count early post-op is usually not pathophysiologically informative for AKI and can reflect transfusion/hemodilution variability. Redundant and low yield for sparse selection.
Platelet maxima are rarely the clinically meaningful signal compared with thrombocytopenia. Adds little unique predictive value for creatinine ratio versus minima/means.
Late platelet maximum is unlikely to capture renal-relevant insult and is often driven by transfusion/recovery patterns. Low unique value and dominated by platelet minima.

Minimum platelet count early can reflect CPB-related consumption, bleeding/transfusion, or early inflammatory activation associated with worse outcomes. Useful but early timing is less reliable for Hour 60 creatinine and may be confounded by perioperative factors.
Platelet nadir at 13–24h better reflects early trajectory of thrombocytopenia/coagulopathy than immediate post-op values. Moderately predictive as a severity marker but still indirect for AKI.

Platelet nadir in the 25–36h window captures persistent/worsening thrombocytopenia closest to the outcome horizon, often tracking systemic inflammation, shock, or consumptive processes linked to AKI risk. Among platelet features, this extreme and timing are most likely to survive sparse selection.
Frequency of platelet checks early post-op is primarily a monitoring/transfusion-practice proxy. Weak direct renal information and likely redundant with actual platelet values.
Platelet measurement frequency reflects clinician concern/bleeding management rather than kidney physiology. Low incremental value for a sparse linear model.
Later platelet measurement frequency may indicate ongoing bleeding/coagulopathy management closer to Hour 60. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by the platelet nadir itself.

Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) reflects RBC indices/anemia phenotype rather than acute renal physiology. Early post-op mean value is unlikely to add unique signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and is dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers.

MCH mean in 13–24h is a stable hematologic index with weak mechanistic linkage to evolving AKI. Low sparse-model priority due to indirectness and likely redundancy with broader anemia/transfusion variables (if present).
MCH mean at 25–36h is closer in time but still a largely non-renal RBC characteristic. Unlikely to survive sparse selection because it adds little unique predictive value for creatinine rise.
Maximum MCH in 0–12h mainly reflects lab variability or transfusion-related shifts, not kidney injury. Extreme aggregation here does not capture a renal insult and is low value for sparse selection.
Peak MCH in 13–24h is not a recognized near-term precursor of AKI and is likely noise/redundant with other hematology indices. Very weak candidate for inclusion in a sparse linear model.

Peak MCH at 25–36h remains an indirect hematologic metric with minimal connection to renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity. Low unique predictive value relative to more direct renal/hemodynamic features.
Minimum MCH in the immediate post-op window is typically driven by measurement variation or dilution/transfusion effects. Not a direct renal signal and unlikely to be selected sparsely.
Low MCH in 13–24h suggests chronic microcytosis/iron deficiency more than acute physiology. Weak and indirect for predicting creatinine_ratio, so low priority.
Minimum MCH at 25–36h is still a chronic RBC characteristic and not an AKI precursor. Redundant/low-yield for sparse selection.
Count of MCH measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than physiology. In 0–12h it is especially routine and unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Measurement frequency reflects clinician behavior/ICU workflow more than kidney injury trajectory. Low sparse-model value and likely confounded by overall illness complexity.
MCH measurement count at 25–36h remains a weak proxy for monitoring rather than a biomarker. Unlikely to outperform direct renal labs or hemodynamic extremes in sparse selection.

Mean sodium in 0–12h reflects fluid balance, dilution, and early dysnatremias that can correlate with severity and renal handling. However it is early and nonspecific, so only a weak-to-moderate sparse candidate.

Mean sodium at 13–24h may capture evolving free-water balance/diuretic effect and overall physiologic stress relevant to AKI risk. Still indirect and often redundant with fluid balance/diuretics, so modest priority.

Mean sodium at 25–36h is the closest window and can reflect ongoing volume status, diuresis, and renal concentrating ability that may precede creatinine rise. More likely to add incremental signal than earlier means, though still indirect.

Peak sodium in 0–12h can indicate hypernatremia from diuresis/insensible losses or hypertonic exposure, which may track with severity and renal stress. But it is early and may be transient, so limited sparse-model priority.

Peak sodium in 13–24h reflects dysnatremia from fluid balance/diuresis rather than direct renal injury. Some association with AKI risk via volume status, but largely indirect and likely redundant with closer-window sodium extremes.

Peak sodium in 25–36h may capture evolving free-water deficit/diuretic effect and concentration from reduced renal water handling closer to the outcome. Among sodium features, this later extreme is the most plausible sparse-model keeper, though still an indirect AKI signal.
Early postoperative nadir sodium is often driven by bypass/fluids and transient shifts. It is noisy for Hour-60 creatinine ratio and dominated by later sodium measures.

Nadir sodium in 13–24h can reflect dilutional hyponatremia from fluid overload, which correlates with illness severity and renal congestion. Still indirect and likely redundant with later-window sodium extremes and direct renal/hemodynamic predictors.

Nadir sodium in 25–36h may indicate ongoing fluid overload/hemodilution closer to Hour 60, which can track renal congestion and AKI trajectory. More temporally relevant than earlier minima, but remains a nonspecific proxy signal.

Count/indicator of sodium measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and routine post-op labs. It is a clinician-behavior proxy with little unique physiologic information for sparse selection.

Sodium measurement frequency in 13–24h is primarily a proxy for acuity or care patterns rather than kidney physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond actual sodium values and other severity markers.

More frequent sodium checks in 25–36h may reflect clinician concern about fluid/electrolyte derangements in a patient trending poorly. Still a proxy variable, but slightly more informative due to proximity to the outcome.

Whether TEG platelet aggregation was measured early post-op reflects bleeding/thrombosis evaluation and transfusion planning. It is not a direct renal signal and is largely a clinician-behavior proxy.
TEG platelet aggregation measurement in 13–24h indicates ongoing hemostasis concerns rather than kidney function. Low priority for sparse modeling of creatinine ratio.

Late TEG platelet aggregation measurement still primarily captures bleeding/coagulopathy management intensity. It has minimal mechanistic linkage to AKI and is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Mean MCHC in 0–12h reflects red cell indices/hemodilution and is typically stable with limited acute renal specificity. Early values are noisy and dominated by more direct perfusion/renal markers.
Mean MCHC in 13–24h is a nonspecific anemia/hemodilution marker and weakly related to renal outcomes. Unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

Mean MCHC in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing hemodilution/transfusion patterns closer to the outcome. Still indirect and generally dominated by direct hemoglobin/hematocrit, fluid balance, and hemodynamic variables.
Max MCHC in 0–12h is usually within a narrow physiologic range and often reflects lab variability rather than a meaningful insult. Very low unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Max MCHC in 13–24h remains a low-variance red cell index with limited pathophysiologic connection to AKI. Redundant and unlikely to be selected in a sparse linear model.

Peak MCHC in 25–36h is mainly an RBC index affected by lab/hemoconcentration artifacts rather than renal physiology. Low unique value for AKI prediction and dominated by more direct perfusion/volume/renal markers.

Early (0–12h) nadir MCHC reflects RBC indices and perioperative dilution/transfusion effects more than evolving kidney injury. Likely noisy and redundant with hematocrit/hemoglobin patterns, so low sparse-model priority.
Minimum MCHC in 13–24h is a nonspecific RBC metric with weak mechanistic linkage to later creatinine rise. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given redundancy and indirectness.

Minimum MCHC closest to the outcome window still primarily captures RBC index variation/measurement artifact rather than renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxic exposure. Adds little unique predictive signal versus hematocrit or direct renal/hemodynamic features.
Count of MCHC measurements is a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than a physiologic biomarker. Early post-op measurement frequency is especially driven by routine labs, so minimal unique value.
Measurement frequency of MCHC in 13–24h reflects clinician behavior/acuity rather than kidney function. In a sparse model it is dominated by actual lab values and hemodynamics.
More frequent MCHC checks in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing instability or bleeding/transfusion workup. Still indirect and behavior-driven, so only a modest score.
Mean hematocrit in 0–12h reflects hemodilution/transfusion and immediate post-bypass fluid shifts. It can relate to oxygen delivery but is early and smoothed, so lower priority than later extremes.

Mean hematocrit in 13–24h captures sustained anemia/hemodilution which can contribute to renal hypoxia, but averaging blunts important lows. Likely redundant with hematocrit_min and later-window values.

Mean hematocrit in 25–36h is closer to Hour-60 and reflects ongoing anemia/hemodilution affecting oxygen delivery and renal vulnerability. Still somewhat redundant with hematocrit_min, but could add signal if anemia is sustained.
Early maximum hematocrit often reflects hemoconcentration immediately post-op and is less tied to AKI risk than anemia/hypoperfusion. Likely noisy and not a sparse-model keeper.
Maximum hematocrit in 13–24h is generally less informative for renal injury than low hematocrit or hypotension/oliguria signals. Adds little unique value relative to min/mean measures.

Later maximum hematocrit may indicate hemoconcentration from diuresis/negative balance, which can correlate with prerenal physiology, but it is indirect. Lower priority than hematocrit_min and direct renal/hemodynamic features.
Early nadir hematocrit captures acute anemia/hemodilution/transfusion needs, which can impair renal oxygen delivery. However 0–12h is prone to transient post-bypass effects, so moderate priority.
Nadir hematocrit in 13–24h better reflects persistent anemia after initial stabilization and can contribute to renal hypoxia. Still somewhat redundant with the closer 25–36h nadir.

Nadir hematocrit in 25–36h is the most temporally proximate and extreme-based anemia signal, relevant to renal oxygen delivery and ongoing bleeding/hemodilution. Among hematocrit variants, this is the strongest sparse-model candidate.

Count of hematocrit checks in the first 12h post-op, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity/transfusion evaluation. Weak, proxy-heavy signal and early window is noisy; unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Frequency of hematocrit measurement in 13–24h, a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy rather than renal physiology. Adds little unique value for creatinine_ratio compared with true hemodynamic/renal markers.

Hematocrit measurement frequency in the closest window (25–36h) may reflect ongoing bleeding/transfusion or instability. Still a monitoring proxy, but timing makes it slightly more informative than earlier epochs.
Mean basophil count in 0–12h, a nonspecific differential component with minimal mechanistic link to AKI. Likely noise and dominated by more relevant inflammatory/renal/hemodynamic features.

Mean basophil count in 13–24h; basophils rarely provide unique predictive information for postoperative creatinine rise. Low priority for sparse models due to weak relevance and redundancy with broader WBC/inflammation signals.
Peak basophil count in 0–12h; extremes here are unlikely to represent a meaningful renal insult. Early post-op lab variability makes this a poor sparse-model candidate.
Peak basophil count in 13–24h; even as an extreme, it is not a recognized driver or early marker of AKI after cardiac surgery. Unlikely to add unique value versus core perfusion/renal biomarkers.
Minimum basophil count in 0–12h, typically reflecting lab noise or dilution rather than kidney injury. Very weak and redundant signal for creatinine_ratio prediction.
Minimum basophil count in 13–24h; not plausibly tied to renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI. Low selection priority in a sparse linear model.
Number of basophil measurements in 0–12h, essentially CBC frequency/monitoring intensity. Proxy feature with little direct renal information and early-window noise.
Basophil measurement frequency in 13–24h, reflecting lab ordering patterns rather than physiology. Unlikely to contribute uniquely once other severity markers are present.
Basophil measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing complications prompting repeated CBCs. Still indirect, but closer timing gives slightly higher potential utility than earlier epochs.
Mean lymphocyte percentage in 0–12h, a nonspecific stress/inflammation marker influenced by hemodilution and steroids. Early timing and indirectness make it a low-priority sparse feature.

Mean lymphocyte percentage in 13–24h may reflect sustained stress response/inflammation associated with complications and AKI risk. Moderately useful but indirect and likely dominated by stronger renal/perfusion variables.
Maximum lymphocyte percentage in 0–12h; extremes are generally preferable, but early post-op variability limits reliability. Provides at best a weak, indirect signal for later creatinine rise.

Peak lymphocyte percentage in 13–24h captures an extreme immune/stress state that may correlate with evolving complications. More informative than the mean within this batch, but still indirect for AKI and not top-tier for sparsity.

Minimum lymphocyte percentage early post-op reflects stress/inflammation but is a nonspecific illness-severity marker. In a sparse model it’s weak and early/noisy for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Minimum lymphocyte percentage in 13–24h may reflect ongoing inflammatory stress, which can correlate with AKI risk indirectly. Still nonspecific and likely dominated by more direct renal/hemodynamic features, so low sparse-selection priority.
Indicator of whether lymphocyte % was measured is primarily a monitoring/ordering-intensity proxy. It adds little direct physiologic information and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement flag reflects clinician behavior/acuity rather than kidney physiology. Redundant as a general intensity-of-care proxy and weak for sparse prediction of creatinine ratio.
Even though closer to the target, this is still just a measurement-frequency proxy rather than a biomarker. Low unique value for a sparse linear model.

Having a random vancomycin level measured suggests exposure to a nephrotoxic drug and/or concern for renal dysfunction. However it’s a clinician-behavior proxy (not dose/level) and early timing limits specificity for Hour-60 creatinine.

Random vanc level measurement in 13–24h may indicate nephrotoxin exposure with emerging renal concern. Still indirect and likely redundant with actual medication dosing/renal labs, so modest priority.

Closer-to-target measurement of a random vanc level can proxy evolving AKI (dose adjustment/monitoring) and nephrotoxin exposure. Among measurement flags, this timing is most informative, but it remains indirect.
Trough measurement implies vancomycin therapy and monitoring for toxicity/renal clearance. Early post-op and still a measurement proxy, so limited unique predictive value.
Trough measured at 13–24h suggests ongoing vancomycin with attention to clearance, indirectly related to AKI risk. Likely redundant with dosing/renal function variables, so low-moderate priority.

Trough measurement in 25–36h is a stronger proxy for impaired clearance or nephrotoxin management near the prediction horizon. Still indirect (no actual level), but among these flags it has higher sparse-model value.

Mean oxygen saturation early post-op reflects respiratory status and overall instability, which can relate to renal hypoxia indirectly. Early averages are often noisy and less specific than hemodynamics for AKI prediction.
Mean SpO2 in 13–24h captures sustained hypoxemia burden, an indirect contributor to renal injury. However it’s a general severity marker and may be redundant with ventilation/hemodynamic variables.

Mean SpO2 in 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and reflects sustained oxygenation adequacy, which can add incremental signal about ongoing organ dysfunction. Still indirect for kidney outcomes, so moderate priority.

Maximum SpO2 mainly reflects periods of adequate oxygenation and is often near ceiling due to supplemental O2. It provides little discriminatory signal and is dominated by mean/min oxygenation metrics.
Max SpO2 in 13–24h is typically ceiling-limited and not informative about hypoxemic burden. Low unique value and redundant compared with mean (or ideally minimum) oxygenation measures.
Represents best oxygenation achieved in the 25–36h window. Max SpO2 is usually near-ceiling and adds little unique signal for AKI risk compared with hypoxemia extremes or hemodynamic/renal markers.
Captures early postoperative hypoxemia episodes. Hypoxemia can contribute to renal injury but 0–12h values are noisy/transient post-bypass and are likely dominated by later trajectory.

Minimum SpO2 in 13–24h reflects ongoing respiratory failure/low oxygen delivery. Some relevance to renal hypoxia, but still indirect and typically weaker than direct renal perfusion/renal labs in sparse selection.

Minimum SpO2 in 25–36h is the closest window and best captures sustained/late hypoxemia that may affect renal oxygen delivery. Among SpO2 features, this extreme is the most informative and least redundant.
Frequency of SpO2 documentation/measurement in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring-intensity proxy. It is not a physiologic biomarker and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Measurement count reflects clinician attention/acuity rather than oxygenation itself. Adds little unique predictive value beyond actual SpO2 minima/maxima.
Later SpO2 measurement frequency is still primarily a care-intensity proxy. Even though closer to outcome, it is indirect and dominated by the actual SpO2 extreme values.

Average ALT in the immediate postoperative period reflects hepatic injury/ischemia or baseline liver disease. It is an indirect severity marker and early means are noisy and redundant with more specific renal/perfusion signals.

Peak ALT in 0–12h can indicate ischemic hepatitis/low-flow state, which correlates with AKI risk. Still indirect and early postoperative peaks may be transient; useful only as a weak low-cardiac-output proxy.
Minimum ALT is generally not clinically meaningful for hypoperfusion or AKI prediction. It is dominated by max/trajectory information and unlikely to add unique value.
ALT measurement frequency is a clinician-behavior/monitoring proxy (e.g., concern for shock or liver injury). Not a direct physiologic signal and low priority for sparse models.
Counts of ALT checks reflect care patterns and illness complexity rather than liver function itself. Provides minimal unique information for predicting creatinine ratio.
Later ALT measurement frequency remains a proxy for clinician concern and is not a biomarker. Unlikely to outperform direct renal labs or hemodynamic extremes in sparse selection.

Mean BUN early post-op reflects baseline renal reserve, catabolism, and early renal hypoperfusion. It is a direct renal-related lab but early values can be confounded by fluids/CPB, so less priority than later BUN.
Mean BUN in 13–24h captures early renal trajectory and evolving azotemia beyond immediate post-op noise. Strong renal-adjacent signal likely to add unique value for predicting later creatinine rise.

Mean BUN in 25–36h is the closest renal-related lab window and reflects evolving azotemia/renal hypoperfusion preceding Hour-60 creatinine. High-priority sparse-model feature with direct mechanistic linkage to AKI risk.
Early peak BUN reflects baseline renal reserve, catabolism, and perioperative volume status but is noisy immediately post-op. Useful signal yet likely dominated by later BUN values closer to Hour 60.

Peak BUN in this window can indicate evolving renal hypoperfusion/AKI or persistent azotemia beyond immediate bypass effects. Stronger sparse-model candidate than 0–12h, though still somewhat redundant with 25–36h BUN.

Late peak BUN is the closest azotemia marker to the Hour 60 creatinine ratio and captures ongoing renal dysfunction/underperfusion. High unique predictive value and likely to survive sparse selection among BUN summaries.
Low BUN early is often driven by hemodilution and fluid shifts after surgery rather than kidney injury. Likely weak and redundant once max/late BUN features are available.
Minimum BUN mainly reflects dilution/nutrition rather than renal injury signal. Adds limited incremental value compared with BUN maxima and later-window measures.
Late minimum BUN can still be influenced by fluid balance and does not capture the ‘injury’ extreme as well as BUN max. Generally dominated by 25–36h BUN max for sparse selection.
Count/frequency of BUN checks is primarily a monitoring-intensity/acuity proxy rather than physiology. Early post-op ordering patterns are especially routine and noisy.
BUN measurement frequency reflects clinician concern and ICU complexity more than renal function itself. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.
More frequent BUN checks late may indicate suspected AKI and could weakly proxy evolving renal issues. Still indirect and typically dominated by the actual BUN values.
Whether amylase was checked early is a clinician-behavior proxy (e.g., concern for pancreatitis/ischemia) with little direct link to creatinine trajectory. Very low priority for sparse selection.
Amylase measurement indicates diagnostic workup rather than renal physiology. Likely sparse-model noise unless no better severity markers exist.
Late amylase measurement (or value recorded as measured) is still largely a workup proxy and not a direct renal signal. Low incremental value versus hemodynamics and renal labs.

Mean pH early reflects acid-base status during immediate post-op instability and ventilation changes. Some association with shock/poor perfusion but timing is noisy and indirect for Hour 60 creatinine.
Sustained acidemia in this window can reflect ongoing hypoperfusion/lactate burden and is a moderate indirect AKI risk marker. Mean may dilute brief insults, limiting sparse-model priority.

Later sustained pH abnormalities are closer to the outcome and can indicate persistent shock/respiratory failure contributing to AKI risk. Stronger indirect predictor and more likely to survive selection than earlier pH means.

Early maximum pH often reflects transient hyperventilation/alkalosis from ventilator management rather than renal-relevant hypoperfusion. Low priority and less informative than low pH or later-window measures.
Represents the highest alkalemia in the 13–24h window. Max pH is usually less informative for AKI risk than acidemia and is largely redundant with pH_min/overall acid-base severity signals.

Represents the highest alkalemia closest to the outcome window (25–36h). Still a weaker renal signal than acidemia and likely reflects ventilation/diuresis practices rather than evolving kidney dysfunction.
Captures early postoperative acidemia, which can reflect hypoperfusion and shock risk. Useful but de-weighted because 0–12h is noisy after bypass/anesthesia and may not persist to Hour 60.

Lowest pH in 13–24h reflects clinically meaningful acidemia from ongoing hypoperfusion/sepsis/respiratory failure. An extreme value is a strong, relatively nonredundant severity signal that can precede AKI.

Lowest pH in 25–36h is the closest available marker of ongoing acidemia near the prediction horizon. This extreme captures sustained/late hypoperfusion or metabolic derangement and is a top sparse-model candidate for predicting creatinine rise.
Counts whether/ how often pH was measured in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and clinician concern. This is a proxy variable and is dominated by actual pH values.
Measurement frequency of pH in 13–24h is primarily a care/acuity proxy rather than physiology. Unlikely to add unique value beyond pH_min/max.
Measurement frequency closest to the outcome may weakly proxy instability prompting repeated ABGs. Still indirect and typically redundant once pH extremes are included.

Whether cholesterol was checked early post-op reflects ordering behavior and baseline risk assessment, not acute renal physiology. Very unlikely to survive sparse selection for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
Cholesterol measurement in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior proxy with minimal mechanistic link to evolving AKI. Adds little unique predictive signal.
Cholesterol measurement in 25–36h remains an ordering/monitoring artifact rather than a renal insult marker. Low priority for a sparse linear model.
Mean lymphocyte count early post-op reflects stress response/inflammation and overall illness severity. It is indirect for AKI and early timing is noisy, so low sparse-model priority.
Mean lymphocyte count in 13–24h is a nonspecific inflammatory/stress marker. Likely redundant with stronger severity/hemodynamic variables and not a direct renal signal.
Maximum lymphocyte count in 0–12h is rarely the clinically relevant extreme (lymphopenia is more informative). Likely noise and dominated by lymphocyte minima/other severity markers.
Maximum lymphocyte count in 13–24h is not a typical AKI precursor and adds little beyond mean/min patterns. Low uniqueness for sparse selection.

Minimum lymphocyte count captures early lymphopenia, a stress/inflammation severity signal that can correlate with complications including AKI. Still indirect and early-window, but the min is the most informative aggregation among lymphocyte features here.

Minimum lymphocyte count reflects stress/inflammation or hemodilution rather than a direct renal signal. It may weakly track illness severity but is unlikely to add unique value for creatinine_ratio in a sparse model.
Measurement frequency is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy, not a physiologic biomarker. Early (0–12h) adds additional noise and is unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Counts how often lymphocytes were checked, reflecting care intensity rather than kidney function. Provides little unique predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Later measurement frequency may indicate evolving complications prompting more labs, but remains an indirect proxy. Slightly more informative due to proximity to Hour 60, yet still low priority for sparsity.
Whether glucose was measured is a routine-care/monitoring proxy and not a direct renal biomarker. In the immediate post-op window it is especially non-specific for later creatinine rise.
Glucose measurement occurrence mainly reflects ICU protocols or perceived instability rather than renal physiology. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.

Later glucose measurement may correlate with ongoing critical illness or insulin infusion, but it is still a proxy variable. Proximity to Hour 60 gives modest potential signal, yet it remains low priority.
CK measurement frequency reflects suspicion for muscle injury/rhabdo or routine panels, not kidney function itself. Early post-op timing and proxy nature make it a weak sparse-model candidate.

Checking CK can indicate concern for rhabdomyolysis/ischemia which can contribute to AKI, but this feature is only the act of measuring. As a clinician-behavior proxy it is still unlikely to be retained if stronger renal/hemodynamic features exist.

Later CK measurement may signal evolving tissue injury with potential renal impact, but it remains indirect and protocol-driven. Some value from temporal proximity, yet still not a strong sparse-selection feature.

Mean eGFR summarizes early post-op renal function derived from creatinine, providing a direct kidney-function signal. However, 0–12h values can be distorted by fluid shifts and are less predictive than later windows.

Mean eGFR at 13–24h reflects early trajectory of renal function after initial post-op noise settles. As a direct renal marker it is a strong sparse-model candidate, though still less proximal than 25–36h.

Mean eGFR at 25–36h is the closest available direct renal-function summary to the Hour-60 outcome and captures evolving AKI. High unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio and likely to survive sparse selection.

Maximum eGFR mainly captures the best (often dilutional) apparent renal function early after surgery rather than injury. It is likely redundant and less informative than later mean eGFR for predicting creatinine rise.
Peak eGFR tends to reflect transient low creatinine/hemodilution rather than renal dysfunction risk. Adds limited unique value beyond mean eGFR in the same epoch.

Even in the later window, maximum eGFR represents the least-impaired moments and is less aligned with AKI prediction than sustained level (mean) or low extremes (not provided). Likely redundant with 25–36h eGFR_mean in a sparse model.

Early post-op nadir eGFR reflects immediate renal dysfunction but is often confounded by bypass-related hemodilution and transient shifts. Useful signal, yet dominated by later (25–36h) eGFR for Hour-60 prediction in a sparse model.
Mid-postop nadir eGFR captures early trajectory toward AKI beyond the immediate post-bypass noise. Strong renal signal, though still somewhat dominated by the closer 25–36h window.

Closest-window nadir eGFR is a near-direct marker of evolving kidney dysfunction and strongly anticipates creatinine at Hour 60. The minimum preserves acute injury signal and is a top sparse-model keeper.
Count/frequency of eGFR measurements is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-concern proxy rather than physiology. Low unique predictive value and likely redundant with actual eGFR values.
Measurement frequency reflects care intensity and suspicion for complications more than renal function itself. Unlikely to survive sparse selection when eGFR_min is available.
Later measurement frequency may weakly proxy evolving instability/AKI workup closer to the target time. Still indirect and typically dominated by the actual eGFR_min value.
LDH measurement frequency is a nonspecific monitoring proxy (hemolysis/ischemia workup) rather than a renal biomarker. Adds little unique value for creatinine_ratio prediction.
LDH measurement count mainly reflects clinician behavior and illness complexity. Weak and indirect for renal outcome, unlikely to be selected sparsely.
Even close to Hour 60, LDH measurement frequency remains a proxy variable without direct renal signal. Low priority versus direct kidney function features.

Average INR early post-op reflects coagulopathy/hepatic dysfunction and transfusion context, which correlate with severity but not specifically renal injury. Mean smooths extremes and is early/noisy, so low sparse priority.

Sustained INR elevation later may indicate ongoing hepatic dysfunction/bleeding/transfusion burden associated with AKI risk. Moderately useful severity signal, but indirect and often redundant with INR_max.

Peak INR in the first 12h captures acute coagulopathy but is frequently transient and influenced by perioperative anticoagulation and dilution. Indirect for renal dysfunction and less informative than later peaks.

Later peak INR captures clinically meaningful coagulopathy/ongoing bleeding or hepatic dysfunction closer to the outcome window. Still an indirect severity marker, but the extreme is the best INR aggregation for sparse inclusion.
Minimum INR is typically near-normal and not a pathophysiologic insult marker. Provides little unique information and is dominated by INR_max/mean.
Minimum INR later is unlikely to reflect risk-relevant extremes and is generally uninformative. Redundant given availability of INR_max/mean.
INR measurement frequency is primarily a proxy for monitoring intensity (bleeding/anticoagulation management). Low direct renal relevance and unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Counts whether INR was checked in 13–24h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity/bleeding concern rather than renal physiology. Weak, proxy-heavy signal and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Whether INR was checked in 25–36h may proxy ongoing instability, anticoagulation management, or hepatic dysfunction, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy, so low priority despite closer timing.

Average eosinophil percentage early post-op is nonspecific and often affected by stress response/steroids. Low direct linkage to AKI and dominated by more proximal/stronger renal-perfusion markers not in this batch.

Mean eosinophil percentage in 13–24h is a nonspecific inflammatory/allergic marker with weak mechanistic connection to post-cardiac surgery AKI. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.

Peak eosinophil percentage could rarely signal drug reaction/atheroembolic phenomena, but in the immediate 0–12h window it is noisy and often steroid-suppressed. Extreme is preferable to mean, yet still low-yield for sparse selection.

Max eosinophil percentage in 13–24h is the most potentially informative eosinophil aggregation (captures spikes), but eosinophilia is an uncommon driver of AKI in this setting. Modest score for possible unique signal, but generally weak.
Minimum eosinophil percentage largely reflects stress leukogram/steroid effect and has little specific renal prognostic content. Also an unhelpful extreme (low eosinophils) for AKI prediction.
Minimum eosinophil percentage in 13–24h is nonspecific and unlikely to capture a renal-relevant insult. Redundant/low-information compared with max if any eosinophil feature were kept.
Indicates whether eosinophils were measured (CBC differential obtained) in 0–12h, mostly routine post-op care. Monitoring-frequency proxy with minimal unique predictive value.
Whether eosinophils were measured in 13–24h reflects lab ordering patterns rather than physiology. Very weak candidate for sparse inclusion.
Measurement indicator in the closest window (25–36h) still primarily captures clinician behavior/acuity rather than a kidney-specific signal. Low priority for sparse modeling.

Whether LDL was checked early post-op is unrelated to acute kidney injury physiology and mostly reflects baseline lipid evaluation or incidental ordering. No meaningful unique value for predicting 60h creatinine ratio.
LDL measurement in 13–24h is a non-renal, non-acute marker and largely administrative/ordering noise. Very unlikely to help a sparse AKI prediction model.
LDL measurement in 25–36h remains clinically disconnected from evolving AKI and is a pure ordering proxy. Should be excluded under sparse feature budgeting.

Mean MCV reflects chronic anemia/nutritional status and transfusion context but is not a direct renal injury marker; early post-op values can be diluted by fluids/transfusions. Weak-to-modest utility as baseline frailty proxy, likely dominated by stronger hemodynamic/renal labs.
MCV in 13–24h is slightly less affected by immediate bypass/fluid shifts than 0–12h and may proxy chronic comorbidity or transfusion burden. Still indirect and low priority for sparse selection.

Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) reflects RBC size and is mainly influenced by baseline hematologic status and transfusion/dilution. The mean value is a weak, indirect signal for AKI and is likely redundant with other severity/bleeding markers, so low sparse-model priority.

Early post-op peak MCV is usually driven by hemodilution/transfusion and lab variability rather than renal physiology. In the 0–12h window it is noisy and unlikely to add unique predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio.

Peak MCV in 13–24h may reflect ongoing transfusion or macrocytosis but remains an indirect correlate of illness/bleeding rather than kidney injury. Slightly more stable than 0–12h, yet still low yield for sparse selection.

Later peak MCV could capture continued transfusion/dilution effects closer to the outcome window. Still a nonspecific hematology signal with limited mechanistic linkage to AKI, so only a weak candidate.
Early minimum MCV is dominated by immediate post-bypass fluid shifts and measurement noise. It is not a direct renal perfusion or kidney function marker and is unlikely to survive sparse screening.

Minimum MCV in 13–24h may reflect dilutional anemia/transfusion patterns but is still far from a direct AKI precursor. Some potential proxy for bleeding/volume status, yet largely redundant and low priority.

Minimum MCV in 25–36h is temporally closer and may weakly proxy ongoing dilution/bleeding management. However it remains nonspecific and likely dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal features, so low-moderate priority.
Count of MCV measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. In the immediate 0–12h period it is routine and unlikely to add unique predictive information.

Measurement frequency reflects care intensity rather than physiology and is not a direct kidney injury signal. It is unlikely to be selected in a sparse linear model when physiologic variables are available.
More frequent MCV checks in 25–36h could indicate ongoing bleeding/transfusion complexity and higher acuity closer to the outcome. Still a proxy feature, so only a modest score.

Mean platelet volume (MPV) can reflect platelet activation/inflammation and perioperative consumption, but it is nonspecific for renal hypoperfusion. Early 0–12h values are noisy post-CPB and low priority for sparse selection.
MPV mean in 13–24h may better reflect evolving inflammation/consumption than immediate post-op labs. Still an indirect severity marker with limited unique value for predicting creatinine ratio.

MPV mean in 25–36h is closer to Hour 60 and may capture sustained inflammatory/consumptive physiology associated with AKI risk. It remains indirect, but among MPV summaries the later mean is the most plausible keeper.
Peak MPV early may reflect acute platelet activation or lab variability after bypass. As an extreme it can be more informative than the mean, but in 0–12h it is still noisy and nonspecific for AKI.

Peak MPV in 13–24h may capture a stronger inflammatory/activation signal than the mean and is less dominated by immediate post-op artifact. It could add some unique prognostic value, though still indirect for renal dysfunction.

Peak MPV in 25–36h is the closest and most extreme aggregation, potentially capturing ongoing platelet activation/inflammation linked to organ dysfunction and AKI risk. Within this batch it is the strongest MPV-derived candidate for sparse inclusion.

Minimum mean platelet volume (MPV) in the immediate post-op window is a nonspecific hematologic/inflammatory marker. It has weak, indirect linkage to AKI and is unlikely to survive sparse selection versus hemodynamic/renal-perfusion signals.

MPV minimum at 13–24h remains a nonspecific marker and is not a direct renal injury or perfusion indicator. Likely low unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio compared with stronger physiologic features.

Later (25–36h) MPV minimum is closer to the outcome window but still reflects nonspecific inflammation/platelet activation rather than kidney function. Could add a small amount of unique signal but is generally a low-priority sparse-model feature.
Count of MPV measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. It is not a physiologic biomarker and is dominated by actual lab values and more kidney-relevant measurements.
MPV measurement frequency in 13–24h mainly reflects care patterns and lab ordering rather than renal pathophysiology. Low priority for sparse selection.
MPV measurement frequency at 25–36h is still a proxy for illness complexity/monitoring rather than a direct AKI precursor. Unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse linear model.

Mean lactate in 0–12h reflects global hypoperfusion/low cardiac output after surgery, which can contribute to AKI risk. However, early post-bypass lactate can be noisy and the mean may dilute short insults, so it’s moderately useful but not top-tier.

Mean lactate at 13–24h captures sustained tissue hypoperfusion and early trajectory, which is more predictive of evolving AKI than immediate post-op values. Strong candidate, though still somewhat redundant with lactate_max in the same epoch.

Peak lactate in 0–12h captures the worst early hypoperfusion/oxygen debt, a plausible precursor to renal injury. Despite early post-op noise, the extreme value preserves insult information better than the mean/min, making it a strong sparse-model candidate.

Peak lactate at 13–24h is a strong marker of ongoing or recurrent hypoperfusion/shock physiology, closely tied to AKI risk and likely to add unique predictive value. Among lactate summaries in this batch, this is the most informative aggregation and timing.
Minimum lactate in 0–12h mostly reflects periods of stability and is less informative about hypoperfusion insults. It is dominated by lactate_max/mean for predicting later creatinine rise.
Minimum lactate at 13–24h may indicate recovery but is generally less predictive of AKI than peak or sustained elevation. Some limited complementary value, but usually redundant in sparse selection.
Lactate measurement frequency is largely a proxy for clinician concern and instability rather than a direct biomarker. It may weakly encode severity but is lower priority than actual lactate levels.
More frequent lactate checks at 13–24h suggest ongoing hemodynamic concern, but this is still a monitoring-intensity proxy. Likely redundant once lactate_max/mean are included.

Lactate measurement frequency at 25–36h may reflect persistent instability closer to the outcome window, but remains an indirect proxy. Low-to-weak incremental value compared with lactate magnitude features.

Mean alkaline phosphatase in 0–12h reflects hepatobiliary/bone sources and is not a direct renal injury marker. Early post-op values are unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio in a sparse model.

Peak alkaline phosphatase early post-op reflects cholestasis/liver/bone signal rather than renal function. In a sparse AKI-focused model it is indirect and likely adds little unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Minimum alkaline phosphatase in the first 12h is not a plausible mechanistic driver or early marker of AKI. Redundant/low-signal for renal prediction and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Counts whether/how often ALP was checked, mainly reflecting monitoring patterns rather than physiology. As a clinician-behavior proxy it has weak, non-specific association with later creatinine changes.

ALP measurement frequency in 13–24h is still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy (e.g., concern for hepatic issues) not a renal biomarker. Low expected unique predictive value for creatinine_ratio.
Later ALP measurement frequency may track complexity of care but remains non-specific and indirect for AKI. Even though closer to Hour 60, it is unlikely to add unique signal in a sparse model.

Mean RDW reflects baseline anemia/inflammation and overall frailty, which can correlate with postoperative complications including AKI. However it is indirect and early-window values are unlikely to add strong unique signal versus later RDW.
RDW mean in 13–24h is a non-specific illness-severity marker and may weakly associate with AKI risk. Still largely redundant with other RDW summaries and not a direct renal perfusion/injury signal.

Later (25–36h) RDW mean may better capture evolving inflammation/hemolysis/transfusion effects that track postoperative trajectory and AKI risk. Among RDW means, this is the most temporally relevant and thus the best sparse-model candidate.

Maximum RDW in 0–12h may reflect preexisting anisocytosis or immediate transfusion/hemolysis effects, but it is non-specific for kidney injury. Early timing and redundancy with later maxima limit sparse-selection priority.
RDW max in 13–24h can capture acute hematologic stress and correlates with severity, but remains an indirect proxy for AKI. Likely dominated by the closer 25–36h extreme if selecting sparsely.

Peak RDW in 25–36h is the most temporally proximate RDW extreme and may capture ongoing transfusion/hemolysis/inflammation that tracks higher AKI risk. Still indirect, but among RDW features this extreme is a reasonable sparse-model keeper.
Minimum RDW is rarely clinically informative and does not represent an acute insult relevant to AKI. Early-window minimum is low-signal and dominated by max/mean summaries.
RDW minimum in 13–24h is not a meaningful physiologic marker for renal dysfunction and is largely noise. Unlikely to contribute unique predictive value in sparse regression.
RDW minimum even in the later window is not an AKI-relevant extreme and is redundant with more informative RDW max/mean. Very low priority for sparse feature selection.
RDW measurement frequency reflects CBC ordering/monitoring intensity rather than a physiologic renal signal. As a proxy variable it is weak and non-specific for predicting creatinine_ratio.
CBC/RDW measurement frequency in 13–24h mainly captures clinician behavior and care intensity. It is not a direct kidney injury marker and is unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

Counts whether RDW was checked in the 25–36h window, reflecting monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. As a clinician-behavior proxy it is unlikely to add unique predictive value in a sparse model.
Average chloride early post-op, influenced by bypass/fluids and transient shifts. Likely redundant with later chloride summaries and less temporally relevant to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Average chloride in 13–24h reflects ongoing fluid/acid-base management but is a weak, indirect AKI signal. Redundant with 25–36h chloride and with extremes that better capture hyperchloremic exposure.

Average chloride closest to the target window, capturing sustained hyperchloremia from saline load/acid-base status that can correlate with AKI risk. Still indirect and often dominated by extreme chloride values, but may survive sparse selection if chloride is informative.

Peak chloride in the immediate post-op period, often driven by intraop/early resuscitation and transient shifts. Extremes are better than means, but early timing makes it noisier and less unique than later peaks.

Peak chloride in 13–24h may reflect ongoing high-chloride fluid exposure and hyperchloremic acidosis, which can associate with AKI. More informative than means, but still indirect and somewhat redundant with the later (25–36h) peak.

Peak chloride in 25–36h is the most temporally proximate extreme and best captures clinically meaningful hyperchloremia near the prediction horizon. Among chloride features, this is the strongest sparse-model candidate due to timing and extreme aggregation.

Lowest chloride early post-op is usually less mechanistically tied to AKI than hyperchloremia and may reflect dilutional effects. Early timing and weaker pathophysiologic link make it a low-priority sparse feature.
Lowest chloride in 13–24h is an indirect and generally weak renal-risk signal compared with high chloride exposure. Likely redundant/noisy and unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.

Lowest chloride in 25–36h is closer to the target but still a weaker and less specific AKI precursor than peak chloride. Could add minor information about dilution/diuresis but is usually dominated by chloride_max/mean.
Number/presence of chloride measurements early post-op mainly reflects monitoring frequency and care patterns. This proxy is unlikely to add unique predictive value beyond actual chloride values.
Measurement frequency of chloride in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy rather than a physiologic marker. Typically redundant with severity signals and not a sparse-model keeper.

Chloride measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing instability or electrolyte/acid-base management. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy and generally dominated by the chloride values themselves.
Whether HDL was checked early post-op reflects ordering behavior and is not a direct renal injury signal. Very unlikely to contribute uniquely to predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
HDL measurement in 13–24h is a low-yield proxy for clinician behavior with minimal mechanistic link to AKI trajectory. Not a sparse-model candidate.
HDL measurement in 25–36h remains a non-physiologic proxy and is rarely informative for near-term creatinine rise. Should be excluded in sparse selection.

Mean neutrophil percentage reflects early postoperative inflammatory/stress response. It is a nonspecific illness-severity marker and likely redundant with later values and other inflammatory labs, so low sparse-model priority.

Average neutrophil percentage in 13–24h captures ongoing inflammation/infection risk. Still indirect for AKI and typically dominated by hemodynamics/renal markers, so unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Peak neutrophil percentage early post-op may indicate acute stress response. Early window is noisy after surgery and the signal is indirect for creatinine rise, so limited unique value.

Higher peak neutrophil percentage at 13–24h may better reflect evolving systemic inflammation/sepsis risk than the mean. Among neutrophil summaries this extreme is the most plausible keeper, but remains indirect for renal dysfunction.

Minimum neutrophil percentage in the immediate post-op period is rarely a meaningful renal-risk signal and may reflect lab variability/dilution. Strongly dominated by other physiologic predictors, so very low priority.
Minimum neutrophil percentage at 13–24h is not a typical driver or early marker of AKI. Adds little unique predictive information compared with max/mean and other ICU features.
Count of neutrophil measurements is mainly a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. It is not a direct physiologic renal signal and is unlikely to be selected in a sparse model.
Measurement frequency reflects care intensity or concern for infection rather than kidney physiology. As a proxy variable it is weak and redundant with other acuity indicators.
Later measurement frequency (25–36h) may proxy evolving complications prompting more labs. Closer timing to Hour 60 gives slightly more potential signal, but it remains indirect and behavior-driven.

Mean albumin early post-op reflects hemodilution, capillary leak, and baseline nutritional/inflammatory status, which correlate with AKI risk. However the 0–12h window is noisy and albumin is indirect, so moderate priority.
Maximum albumin in 0–12h is less informative than low values for capillary leak/hemodilution and is sensitive to fluid shifts. Likely redundant with mean/min and not a strong sparse-model keeper.

Minimum albumin in 0–12h captures the worst hemodilution/capillary leak state, which can track severity and renal hypoperfusion risk. Within the albumin family, the extreme low is the most informative aggregation despite early-window noise.
Albumin measurement count is primarily a proxy for clinician ordering/monitoring intensity. It provides little direct physiologic information and is unlikely to add unique predictive value.
Frequency of albumin checks reflects practice patterns (e.g., nutrition/volume assessment) more than renal trajectory. As a proxy feature it is low priority for sparse selection.
Later albumin measurement frequency may indicate ongoing instability or fluid management concerns closer to the outcome window. Still largely a behavior proxy, so only modest potential value.
Whether triglycerides were measured is a clinician-behavior proxy (e.g., propofol monitoring) and not a direct AKI precursor. Early timing and indirectness make it a very weak sparse-model candidate.

Whether triglycerides were checked in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring/TPN/pancreatitis workup proxy rather than renal physiology. Very unlikely to add unique value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio in a sparse model.

Whether triglycerides were checked in 25–36h reflects clinician behavior/complexity more than kidney injury. Even though closer to the target, it is still a weak, indirect proxy and low priority for sparse selection.

Average hemoglobin in the immediate post-op window reflects hemodilution/bleeding/transfusion and oxygen delivery. Early means are noisy and largely redundant with later or extreme anemia signals for predicting AKI trajectory.

Mean hemoglobin at 13–24h captures ongoing anemia/hemodilution but smooths short critical lows. Likely dominated by hemoglobin_min (especially later) and by direct perfusion/renal markers not in this batch.

Mean hemoglobin at 25–36h reflects sustained anemia closer to the prediction horizon, which can contribute to renal hypoxia risk. Still somewhat redundant with hemoglobin_min, but timing makes it a moderate sparse-model candidate.

Peak hemoglobin in 0–12h often reflects hemoconcentration or transfusion effects and is not a direct AKI precursor. It is a weak and noisy signal early after surgery and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Maximum hemoglobin in 13–24h is generally less informative for renal risk than anemia extremes. Mostly redundant/low-yield compared with hemoglobin_min or later-window summaries.
Maximum hemoglobin in 25–36h is not a strong mechanistic driver of creatinine rise and often reflects transfusion/diuresis rather than injury. Low unique predictive value for a sparse linear model.

Lowest hemoglobin in 0–12h captures acute anemia/bleeding and potential renal hypoxia risk. However, early post-op values are confounded by bypass/hemodilution and may be less predictive than later minima.

Lowest hemoglobin in 13–24h reflects clinically meaningful anemia after initial stabilization and can signal ongoing bleeding/transfusion burden affecting renal perfusion/oxygen delivery. As an extreme, it is more informative than mean/max and a strong sparse-model candidate.

Lowest hemoglobin in 25–36h is the closest and most pathophysiologically relevant anemia extreme for subsequent AKI/creatinine rise. Among hemoglobin aggregations, this is the highest-yield, least redundant signal for sparse selection.
Frequency of hemoglobin checks in 0–12h is mainly a proxy for bleeding concern/instability and ICU intensity. It may weakly correlate with AKI risk but is indirect and behavior-driven.

Hemoglobin measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects ongoing monitoring for bleeding/transfusion needs rather than renal physiology. Modest proxy value but unlikely to beat actual hemoglobin extremes in a sparse model.

Hemoglobin measurement frequency in 25–36h may indicate continued instability or transfusion, indirectly associated with AKI risk. Still a clinician-behavior proxy and typically dominated by hemoglobin_min/mean values.
Whether TEG fibrinogen activity was measured early is a coagulation/bleeding-management proxy. It is not a direct renal signal and is unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

TEG fibrinogen activity measurement in 13–24h indicates ongoing coagulopathy/bleeding evaluation rather than kidney injury. As a monitoring/procedure proxy, it is low priority for sparse feature selection.

Indicates whether TEG fibrinogen activity was checked (monitoring/bleeding-management intensity). It is a clinician-behavior proxy with weak direct linkage to AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection.

Average ACT in the immediate post-op window reflects anticoagulation/heparin reversal and bypass-related management. Mean values are less informative than extremes and are largely indirect for renal injury, so low sparse-model priority.

Peak ACT early post-op may capture excessive anticoagulation or delayed reversal. Still mostly a procedural/management signal rather than renal physiology and early timing is noisy, so only weakly useful.

Lowest ACT early post-op mainly reflects heparin reversal/protamine effect and lab variability. It is indirect for kidney dysfunction and dominated by more direct hemodynamic/renal markers, so very low priority.
Frequency of ACT checks reflects monitoring intensity around surgery rather than physiology. As a measurement-count proxy it adds little unique predictive value for creatinine at 60h.
ACT measurement frequency in 13–24h suggests ongoing anticoagulation concerns or procedures. This is still primarily clinician behavior and not a direct AKI precursor, so low priority.
ACT measurement frequency closer to the outcome may indicate ongoing anticoagulation/ECMO/IABP-type complexity. Slightly more informative due to proximity, but remains an indirect monitoring proxy.
Mean AST early post-op reflects hepatic/ischemic injury and overall shock burden. It is an indirect severity marker and the mean smooths peaks, so limited unique value for sparse selection.

Peak AST in 0–12h can capture ischemic hepatitis/low-flow states that also predispose to AKI. Among AST aggregations, the max best preserves the insult signal, but early timing and indirectness keep it moderate.
Minimum AST is not clinically meaningful for organ injury and is largely noise around baseline. It is dominated by max/trajectory signals and offers little predictive value.
AST measurement count reflects monitoring intensity rather than hepatic injury magnitude. As a proxy variable it is unlikely to add unique predictive information for creatinine_ratio.
Frequency of AST checks in 13–24h is mainly clinician behavior/acuity proxy. Without the actual AST values, it is weak and redundant with better severity markers.
AST measurement frequency closer to Hour 60 may reflect evolving organ dysfunction prompting repeat labs. Still a monitoring proxy and inferior to actual AST extremes, so only modest priority.

Mean total protein early post-op reflects hemodilution, capillary leak, and nutritional/inflammatory status. It is indirect for AKI and early values are heavily confounded by fluids, so low-to-moderate utility.
Maximum total protein in the first 12h is rarely a meaningful renal-risk signal and may reflect lab/volume status noise. It is unlikely to contribute uniquely in a sparse model.

Minimum total protein early post-op can capture marked hemodilution/capillary leak and low oncotic pressure, which correlate with fluid overload and AKI risk. Within this family, the minimum is the most informative extreme, though still indirect and early.

Count/frequency of total protein labs in 0–12h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. Low unique value for sparse AKI prediction and dominated by direct renal/hemodynamic signals.

Measurement frequency of total protein in 13–24h is a clinician-behavior proxy (workup/acuity) not a kidney function marker. Unlikely to survive sparse selection given indirectness and redundancy with stronger severity indicators.

Later (25–36h) monitoring frequency may weakly reflect evolving complications prompting labs, closer to the 60h target. Still a proxy variable and typically dominated by actual lab values and urine output/hemodynamics.

Whether HbA1c was checked early post-op reflects baseline diabetes assessment/documentation rather than acute renal trajectory. As a measurement flag it adds little unique predictive signal for 60h creatinine ratio.
HbA1c measurement in 13–24h is still a workup/administrative proxy and not a physiologic AKI precursor. Sparse models would prefer actual diabetes/comorbidity variables over this indicator.
HbA1c measurement by 25–36h remains a clinician-behavior marker with minimal direct linkage to near-term creatinine rise. Very low priority versus direct renal perfusion and kidney function features.

Mean serum CO2/bicarbonate in 0–12h reflects acid–base status influenced by bypass, ventilation, and fluids. Early averages are noisy and less specific for evolving AKI, so low sparse-selection priority.
Mean CO2 in 13–24h captures developing metabolic acidosis that can accompany hypoperfusion/AKI. Useful but somewhat nonspecific and often less informative than extremes in the later window.

Mean CO2 in 25–36h is closer to the 60h outcome and reflects sustained acid–base derangement that may track renal dysfunction. Still indirect and potentially redundant with lactate/creatinine/urine output if available.
Maximum CO2 in 0–12h often reflects transient alkalosis/ventilation effects rather than kidney injury. Early max is unlikely to add unique predictive value for 60h creatinine ratio.
Higher CO2 max in 13–24h may indicate metabolic alkalosis (diuresis, chloride loss) and is not a strong AKI precursor. Generally weaker and less directly linked than low CO2 (acidosis) signals.
CO2 max in 25–36h is closer in time but still represents alkalosis/compensation more than renal hypoperfusion. Likely redundant and lower yield than CO2 minima for AKI prediction.
Minimum CO2 in 0–12h can capture early metabolic acidosis from shock/hypoperfusion, a plausible AKI precursor. However, immediate post-op physiology is noisy, limiting sparse-model priority.

Minimum CO2 in 13–24h captures clinically meaningful acidosis that often reflects ongoing hypoperfusion/sepsis/renal dysfunction. Extremes preserve insult signal and are more likely to add unique predictive value than means/max.

Minimum CO2 in 25–36h is the closest extreme acid–base marker to the 60h creatinine outcome and strongly reflects ongoing metabolic acidosis associated with evolving AKI. High-yield, nonredundant signal likely to survive sparse selection within this batch.

Frequency of CO2 measurements in 0–12h is mainly a monitoring/acuity proxy (ventilation changes, instability). Slightly informative for severity but indirect and dominated by the actual CO2 values, especially later minima.

Count/frequency of CO2 measurements in 13–24h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity or ventilator/acid–base concern. Weak, indirect proxy and unlikely to add unique value for AKI prediction in a sparse model.
Frequency of CO2 checks in 25–36h may reflect evolving instability closer to the outcome window. Still largely clinician-behavior/acuity proxy rather than renal physiology, so low priority.
Whether BNP was measured early post-op, reflecting clinician concern for heart failure/volume status. Mostly a behavior proxy and very early/noisy relative to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

BNP measurement flag in 13–24h indicates monitoring/diagnostic workup rather than a physiologic BNP value. Adds little unique renal signal and is dominated by direct hemodynamics/renal markers (not present here).

BNP measurement flag closer to Hour 60 may correlate with persistent congestion/low output states that can contribute to AKI. Still indirect and redundant with better physiologic variables, so only modest priority.

Average PaCO2 in the immediate post-op window reflects ventilation and acid–base status but is often transient/noisy after anesthesia/CPB. Mean aggregation further dilutes extremes and is unlikely to be selected sparsely for creatinine ratio.

Mean PaCO2 in 13–24h may capture sustained hypoventilation/hypercapnia and overall illness severity. Still an indirect renal predictor and likely redundant with more informative extremes in later windows.

Mean PaCO2 in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect ongoing respiratory failure/acid–base burden associated with multi-organ dysfunction. However it remains indirect and typically weaker than extreme PaCO2 summaries for sparse selection.

Peak PaCO2 early post-op can indicate acute hypoventilation or severe respiratory compromise. Early timing and potential transient effects reduce reliability for Hour-60 creatinine ratio, but extremes are more informative than means.

Peak PaCO2 in 13–24h captures episodes of significant hypercapnia that may track with severity, hemodynamic stress, and evolving organ dysfunction. More informative than mean/min and less noisy than 0–12h, so moderate sparse-model value.

Peak PaCO2 in 25–36h is the closest extreme measure and best captures late, clinically meaningful hypercapnic episodes reflecting ongoing critical illness. Among PaCO2 features, this is the most likely to survive sparse selection as a unique severity/trajectory signal.
Minimum PaCO2 early post-op often reflects transient hyperventilation/ventilator settings rather than pathologic renal risk. Early window and weaker mechanistic link to AKI make it low priority.

Minimum PaCO2 in 13–24h may indicate aggressive ventilation or metabolic acidosis compensation, but it is a less direct and less specific signal than hypercapnic extremes. Likely redundant with other severity markers.

Minimum PaCO2 in 25–36h is closer to outcome and may reflect compensation for metabolic acidosis, but it is still nonspecific and often ventilator-driven. Lower priority than pCO2_max for capturing clinically important extremes.
Frequency of PaCO2 measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects routine post-op ABG monitoring and ventilator management. Strongly behavior-driven and early, so minimal unique predictive value.

Count of PaCO2 measurements in 13–24h is a monitoring-intensity proxy that correlates with acuity but not directly with renal physiology. Unlikely to be retained in a sparse model when actual PaCO2 extremes are available.
Count/frequency of PaCO2 measurements in 25–36h is mainly a monitoring-intensity/ventilation-management proxy. It is indirect for AKI and unlikely to add unique value in a sparse model.

Whether indirect bilirubin was checked in the immediate 0–12h window reflects clinician behavior/concern more than renal physiology. Early post-op testing patterns are noisy and weakly specific for later creatinine rise.

Indirect bilirubin measurement in 13–24h is still primarily a surveillance proxy (hemolysis/hepatic workup) rather than a kidney signal. Low likelihood of surviving sparse selection versus direct hemodynamic/renal markers.

Indirect bilirubin measurement in 25–36h may indicate ongoing hemolysis/hepatic dysfunction or complexity, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy and not a direct renal predictor, so only modest priority.

Average calcium in 0–12h reflects early post-bypass dilution/transfusion and replacement practices. Mean values in this early window are often transient and are dominated by later/extreme calcium signals for sparse prediction.

Mean calcium in 13–24h may track ongoing critical illness and citrate load from transfusion, but it is nonspecific for kidney injury. Likely redundant with min/max calcium and later (25–36h) summaries.

Mean calcium in 25–36h captures more sustained electrolyte derangement closer to the Hour-60 outcome and may reflect ongoing organ dysfunction. Still relatively nonspecific and potentially redundant with calcium extremes, so moderate priority.
Peak calcium in 0–12h is often driven by supplementation and peri-bypass shifts rather than evolving AKI. Early maxima are noisy and unlikely to add unique predictive value.

Peak calcium in 13–24h may reflect aggressive replacement or metabolic shifts but is not a strong mechanistic precursor of AKI. Less informative than minima and later-window features for sparse selection.

Peak calcium in 25–36h is closer to the target but typically reflects treatment effects more than renal hypoperfusion. Provides limited unique AKI signal compared with calcium minima or direct renal/hemodynamic variables.

Lowest calcium in 0–12h can indicate citrate effect, hemodilution, or severe illness, which may correlate with AKI risk. However, early post-op lows are common/transient and are dominated by later minima.

Lowest calcium in 13–24h may better reflect persistent derangement (transfusion burden, shock physiology) associated with AKI risk. More informative than mean/max but still indirect for kidney function.

Lowest calcium in 25–36h captures a late extreme electrolyte abnormality closest to Hour 60, often marking ongoing critical illness/transfusion burden and physiologic stress linked to AKI. Among calcium features, this extreme and timing make it the strongest sparse-model candidate.
Frequency of calcium checks in 0–12h is largely routine post-cardiac surgery monitoring and clinician behavior. It is a weak, nonspecific proxy and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Number of calcium measurements in 13–24h reflects monitoring intensity rather than a physiologic renal signal. Adds little unique information beyond actual calcium values/extremes.

More frequent calcium checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing instability or replacement needs, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a proxy variable and generally dominated by the calcium min/mean values themselves.

Mean PTT in the immediate post-op window reflects anticoagulation/coagulopathy and transfusion milieu more than renal physiology. Early mean values are noisy and largely redundant with later/extreme PTT summaries for sparse AKI prediction.

Mean PTT at 25–36h captures ongoing anticoagulation/coagulopathy and illness complexity. It is a weak, indirect AKI signal and likely dominated by more direct renal/hemodynamic features; mean also dilutes extremes.

Peak PTT early post-op may indicate high heparin effect, bleeding risk, or hepatic dysfunction. It is indirect for creatinine rise and early timing is confounded by bypass/heparin reversal, so low sparse-model priority.

Peak PTT at 25–36h can reflect persistent coagulopathy/anticoagulation and systemic dysfunction closer to the outcome window. Among PTT variants, the late maximum best captures an extreme abnormality, but remains an indirect AKI predictor.

Minimum PTT early post-op is usually less pathophysiologically informative (often normal/low due to measurement variability or reversal). It offers little unique value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio and is dominated by later/extreme abnormalities.

Minimum PTT at 25–36h is typically not the clinically relevant direction for renal risk compared with elevated PTT. It is unlikely to survive sparse selection given weak mechanistic linkage and redundancy with max/mean.
Count of PTT measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior proxy. Early post-op measurement frequency is routine and adds minimal unique predictive signal for creatinine ratio.
PTT measurement frequency at 13–24h reflects care complexity (e.g., anticoagulation management) rather than kidney function. As a proxy variable it is low priority for a sparse linear model.

More frequent PTT checks at 25–36h may indicate ongoing anticoagulation issues or complications, which can correlate with downstream AKI risk. Still largely a clinician-behavior proxy, so only modest sparse-model value.

Mean monocyte count early post-op reflects inflammatory response and hemodilution effects. It is nonspecific and early timing is noisy, making it unlikely to add unique predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Mean monocyte count at 13–24h is a general inflammation marker that may weakly track systemic stress. It is indirect for AKI and likely redundant with stronger inflammatory/illness-severity markers, so low priority.

Peak monocyte count in the first 12h is dominated by perioperative stress and lab variability. It has little direct linkage to renal hypoperfusion or evolving AKI and is unlikely to be selected sparsely.

A higher monocyte peak at 13–24h may reflect sustained inflammation, which can correlate with complications. However it remains nonspecific and typically weaker than direct renal/hemodynamic predictors, so modest value.
Minimum monocyte count early post-op is often driven by dilution/leukocyte shifts and is not a clear renal-risk signal. It is unlikely to contribute uniquely in sparse selection.
Minimum monocyte count at 13–24h is not a well-established predictor of AKI compared with other labs/vitals. It is nonspecific and redundant, so very low sparse-model priority.
Monocyte measurement count is a proxy for CBC ordering frequency and monitoring intensity. It does not represent a physiologic renal signal and is unlikely to survive sparse feature selection.
Count of monocyte labs obtained (monitoring intensity) in 13–24h. Mostly a clinician-behavior proxy with weak, indirect linkage to AKI and unlikely to survive sparse selection.
Count of monocyte labs obtained in 25–36h. Despite closer timing, it remains primarily a monitoring/acuity proxy rather than a renal physiology signal.
